document_name: Botswana Private Occupational Health Service Standards and Criteria

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service's clinical and managerial leaders are identified and are collectively responsible for defining the occupational health service's mission and creating the plans and policies needed to fulfil the mission.
standards.intent:
It is important that all members of the occupational health service team are recognised and included in the process of defining the service's mission. An occupational health service's mission statement usually reflects the needs of its employee/client population and clinical services are designed and planned to respond to those needs.
Effective leadership is essential for an occupational health service to be able to operate efficiently and fulfil its mission. Leadership is provided by individuals working together or separately and can be provided by any number of individuals.
The leaders of the practice determine what primary care services are essential to the community, ideally in collaboration with the community, as well as the scope and intensity of these services.
A strategic plan outlining the proposed development of the occupational health service over the coming year is a useful tool to support the service in achieving its mission and meeting identified employee/client needs. To ensure effective implementation, the plan should be revisited regularly throughout the year to document progress against agreed, predetermined, time-bound targets. The occupational health service's strategic plan should be reviewed yearly to ensure that it remains reflective of the current needs of the occupational health service population.

criteria.criterion_text: The leaders of the occupational health service are formally or informally identified.
criteria.guideline: Where an occupational health service consists of more than two practitioners the leaders of the occupational health service must be formally identified.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has a mission statement that reflects its strategic objectives and matches the needs of the community served.
criteria.guideline: The mission statement is critical to the strategic planning process since it provides clear, guiding principles that define who the healthcare provider is as an organisation and why the occupational health service exists. Mission statements create the foundation for action planning and a basis for accountability with the community.

criteria.criterion_text: The leaders are collectively responsible for ensuring that the mission statement is known to all personnel, employee/clients, carers and the community served.
criteria.guideline: This can be achieved in many ways, such as, by public display of the mission statement on notice boards, in the waiting area or included in printed information leaflets given to each employee/client when they first visit the occupational health service. If the occupational health service has a website, the mission statement can also be published on the website. Documented evidence that personnel have read the mission statement must be provided for full compliance with this criterion.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service participates in community health activities.
criteria.guideline: The mission of a practice should meet the needs of the community. To be compliant with this criterion evidence must be provided of interaction with members of the health committees and participation in community health activities.

criteria.criterion_text: The leaders, in liaison with the whole occupational health service team, coordinates the compilation of an annual strategic plan and budget.
criteria.guideline: This criterion requires all departments within the occupational health service to be involved in the compilation of the annual strategic plan and budget to ensure that the needs of each department are discussed, considered and met if possible within the scope of the occupational health service budget. Compliance will be demonstrated by documented evidence, for example, minutes of meetings.

criteria.criterion_text: The leaders are collectively responsible for implementing the occupational health service’s mission and strategic plan.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of an action plan for achieving the occupational health service’s mission and strategic plan will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: The strategic plan is evaluated on an annual basis.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by providing documented evidence that the service’s strategic plan has been evaluated to ensure that it remains reflective of the current needs of the patient population.

criteria.criterion_text: Regular monitoring of the implementation of the strategic plan against envisaged timeframes and progress in achieving its objectives is documented at intervals determined by the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: This criterion requires that occupational health service leaders adhere to the timeframes that were set at the beginning of each year for achieving the strategic plan. For any plan to be implemented effectively, it is essential to monitor the progress that is being made towards achieving the outcomes that have been planned. This criterion further requires the occupational health service to document the progress that is being made during the year towards achieving the strategic plan.

criteria.criterion_text: Where appropriate, the leadership roles in various positions are documented, agreed to and known by the personnel.
criteria.guideline: This may, for example include personnel responsible for monitoring infection control occupational health services, resuscitation, stock control, employee/client files etc.

standards.standard_title: A manager is responsible for operating the occupational health service within relevant laws and regulations.
standards.intent: The occupational health service manager is appointed to be responsible for the overall, day-to-day operation of the occupational health service. These responsibilities are documented and known to the occupational health service personnel. The occupational health service manager is responsible for promoting and monitoring the implementation of the policy and procedure framework of the occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: The manager is responsible for the day-to-day running of the occupational health service.
The manager has the education and/or experience necessary to carry out his or her responsibilities.
criteria.guideline: If the occupational health service manager is a solo practitioner or partner in the occupational health service this criterion will be scored based on the evidence of effective management found throughout the occupational health service during the survey. If the occupational health service manager is an employee/client of the occupational health service, compliance will be assessed against the requirements set out in the position description.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service is licensed in terms of relevant legislation for the level and type of services provided.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: The manager ensures that there is a system in place to monitor the implementation of applicable laws and regulations.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will be scored compliant by default, although a PC rating is given whenever there is definite evidence of non-adherence to any legal requirement. In these cases, the transgression needs to be recorded in detail to motivate for the PC rating and it should be based on accurate facts. Common examples of non-conformance with legal requirements include the following deficiencies: The required certificates are not available, for example, fire clearance certificates, electrical installation certificates, commissioning certificates (for example, ethylene oxide sterilisers), pressure test certificates for vessels under pressure Internal/external financial audits are not conducted Nurses transcribe doctors’ prescriptions and/or dispense medication Pharmaceutical items are incorrectly labelled Proof of current registration of professional personnel with the relevant councils is not available In order to demonstrate compliance, occupational health services may wish to compile a template listing the criteria requiring demonstration of compliance with country-specific legislation and regulations against the relevant national acts, regulations, etc. applicable to the criterion.

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence of response to any reports from inspecting and regulatory authorities.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  This requires documented evidence for whatever inspections may have been conducted. Examples may include inspections by the following authorities The local fire authority for fire clearance purposes The national authority responsible for radiation safety aspects The national authority responsible for health and safety compliance The national authority responsible for financial compliance The national authority responsible for maintenance requirements such as boiler inspections and pressure tests, autoclave pressure tests The local authority responsible for testing of water supplies The relevant professional bodies where student training is provided Licensing authorities for facilities such as the pharmacy, etc. Licensing authorities for facilities. Immigration requirements for non-resident

criteria.criterion_text: The manager implements processes to manage and control human, financial and other resources.
criteria.guideline: The criterion will be scored after the final assessment of Management and Leadership criteria and will be scored according to the findings of financial and human resource management and other criteria dealing with adequate supply and effective management of resources (medication, consumables, equipment etc.).  Linked criteria: 1.4.1.1-  1.4.1.3 1.4.2.1-  1.4.2.4 1.4.3.1-  1.4.3.4 1.5.1.1-  1.5.2.4 1.5.2.1- 1.5.2.4 1.5.3.1- 1.5.3.6 1.6.1.1- 1.6.1.4

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service facilitates communication between teams and individuals within the occupational health service.
standards.intent: The leaders develop a culture that emphasises cooperation and communication. Relevant personnel members become part of the communication network.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service leaders facilitate communication between teams where relevant and between individual personnel members.
criteria.guideline: ROOT "Teams" refer to all the groups of employee/clients and practitioners involved in the occupational health service, for example administration, cleaning and clinical personnel. Evidence of for example, minutes of personnel meetings, memos and communication tools can be provided as evidence. There must be evidence of at least quarterly personnel meetings.

criteria.criterion_text: Agendas for meetings are prepared and shared in order to allow those attending to prepare for participation.
criteria.guideline: An assessment of a sample of agendas of meetings should be undertaken to determine the trend and "general" compliance as the criterion should not be penalised on one or two exceptions.

criteria.criterion_text: Minutes of meetings are taken and circulated to all relevant personnel.
criteria.guideline: An assessment of a sample of minutes of meetings should be undertaken to determine the trend and "general" compliance as the criterion should not be penalised for one or two exceptions. The manner in which minutes are circulated should be assessed and should consist of documented evidence of the circulation and acknowledgment of receipt.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a procedure to make sure that important matters resulting from management meetings are communicated to and acted upon by personnel.
criteria.guideline: The existing "mechanism" should be assessed for effectiveness in conveying this information to personnel but surveyors must further note the importance of searching for evidence of "and acted upon". This generally requires that minutes should reflect the allocation of responsibilities for carrying out tasks and that these are reported on at subsequent meetings.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service engages with a range of health, community and disability services to plan and facilitate optimal clinical care.
standards.intent:
Coordination of care for individuals, families and communities is part of the accepted definition of medical care. For employee/clients with complex care needs, for example, frail elderly, severe disabilities or multiple comorbidities, occupational health services are encouraged to coordinate clinical care with other health services including allied health and pharmacy as well as social, disability and community services.
It is important for occupational health services to identify relevant services within the local area that can enhance clinical care, to have updated registers of such services at hand and to build sound working relationships with these service providers to facilitate good, collaborative care.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service plans and coordinates comprehensive care by establishing relations and maintaining contact with other relevant services and agencies, including both governmental and non-governmental agencies.
criteria.guideline: The purpose of this criterion is to ensure that practitioners are aware of members of the multidisciplinary team in both the private and government sector and engage them when required to ensure that the employee/client receives the best possible care. Documented evidence of such networking (for example, minutes of the meetings or email correspondence) should be made available for assessment.

criteria.criterion_text: Information on services, hours of operation and processes for obtaining care is provided to services in the community who work in collaboration with the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: This information needs to be provided for example, to community nursing teams, step down care, allied health professionals etc. Compliance can be demonstrated by for example, email correspondence with the various services or by providing information pamphlets about the practice to these services.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service determines that the receiving individual/organisation can meet the employee/client’s continuing care needs and establishes arrangements to ensure continuity.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service participates in the development of necessary reaction teams in relation to district health measures.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service manager ensures that policies and procedures which support the activities of the occupational health service are implemented.
standards.intent: The practice manager ensures that all policies and procedures which apply to each team within the practice are available to personnel, and are implemented and monitored.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures that guide and support the different services offered by the occupational health service are implemented.
criteria.guideline: The criterion will be scored at the end of the survey. If this criterion is scored PC or NC Surveyors must document examples of non-compliance. This criterion will automatically be scored NC if there is evidence of non-compliance with policies and procedures that affect employee/client and personnel safety.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures are signed and dated by persons authorised to do so.
criteria.guideline: The initial implementation date and the name, designation and signature of the person authorising the policy must be clearly legible on each policy and procedure.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures are correctly compiled, indexed and filed.
criteria.guideline: • Title: Each policy should have a name or title for identification and reference Identifying number: This is to facilitate indexing, locating the policy when required and communicating about the policy accurately Policy statement: What is to be achieved and why Procedure: How the policy is to be achieved. This is a step-by-step description of what needs to be done (like a recipe – following each step should result in a predictable outcome irrespective of who follows the steps) Associated references: This is required when the policy is directly associated with specific laws, regulations or policies or corporate policies and procedures Dates: The date of the original policy, the date(s) of any revision(s) and the date of the next planned review Signature: This is the signature of the person identified and authorised to approve policies and procedures If policies and procedures are only available electronically, all personnel must be able to demonstrate how to access the documents.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures are reviewed according to occupational health service policy and then dated and signed.
criteria.guideline: Policies and procedures must be reviewed at least every 5 years and when changes in evidence based occupational health service requires earlier review.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a process to ensure that personnel are familiar with relevant policies and procedures.
criteria.guideline: Compliance with this criterion can be achieved by means of, for example, signature sheets attached to each policy and procedure or by attendance registers at in-service training relating to policies and procedures.

standards.standard_title: There is a plan for the provision of adequate numbers of suitably qualified personnel.
standards.intent:
It is advisable for the occupational health service to plan and implement uniform programmes and processes related to the recruitment, retention and development of all personnel. Personnel retention rather than recruitment provides greater long-term benefit. Retention is increased when leaders support personnel development.
The occupational health service has a written plan which identifies the numbers and types of personnel required and the skills, knowledge and other requirements needed in each team.
The planning process includes:
Personnel recruitment
Numbers and categories of personnel required
Desired education, qualifications, skills and knowledge
Personal development of personnel
Personnel retention

criteria.criterion_text: There are documented processes for staffing the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: Compliance with this criterion will require the practitioner to show documented evidence that clinical work load and acuity were studied and considered when planning the staffing for the occupational health service. This must be evident for both the number of personnel required as well as their qualifications.

criteria.criterion_text: The desired education, qualifications, skills and knowledge are defined for personnel.
criteria.guideline: These requirements must be discussed and agreed by all relevant personnel during the recruitment process and form part of the position description.

criteria.criterion_text: The processes include measures to improve personnel retention.
criteria.guideline: Retention of personnel as opposed to the recruitment of new personnel is more cost effective and more likely to result in better clinical care due to improved continuity of care and service provision and the accumulation of experience. It is therefore strategically important for retention to be considered as part of the recruitment process. As an additional benefit, measures taken to improve retention are likely to improve personnel satisfaction, and satisfied personnel provide better services. For an occupational health service to be compliant with this criterion evidence must be provided of at least a personnel satisfaction survey.

criteria.criterion_text: Where occupational health services are not led by a qualified consultant in occupational medicine, the occupational health service has arrangements in place to receive advice from one.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by providing documented evidence of such arrangements and consultations.

standards.standard_title: There is an effective process for gathering, verifying and evaluating the credentials (registration, education, training and experience) of those health care professionals who are permitted to provide occupational health services independently.
standards.intent:
The occupational health service needs to ensure that it has qualified healthcare professional personnel who appropriately match its mission, resources and employee/client needs.
An individual's credentials consist of an appropriate current registration, evidence of completion of professional education and any additional training and experience. There is a process for gathering this information and verifying its accuracy. The process applies to all clinical personnel employed by the occupational health service, including locums.

criteria.criterion_text: There is implementation of a reliable, documented process for evaluating and verifying the credentials of all health professionals.
criteria.guideline: This can be achieved, for example, by having a standardised checklist of requirements that must be verified. This should include reference checks. The completed document can then be retained in the employee/client's personnel file. Compliance will be verified during an audit of the personnel files of various categories of professional personnel.

criteria.criterion_text: Personnel files contain copies of qualifications and licences/registration from the relevant authority for all health professionals.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Copies of all the relevant original degrees/diplomas/certificates must be available, as well as evidence of registration with the relevant registration bodies. Compliance will be verified during an audit of the personnel files.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a system to track the annual registration of all health professionals.
criteria.guideline: The system must reflect the date that each category of health professional's licence is due for renewal and document that the original, renewed licence has been seen.

criteria.criterion_text: All personnel with direct contact with the public have had a police check, a copy of which is kept in their personnel file.
criteria.guideline: Police checks are repeated every 3 years or as appropriate. Compliance will be verified during an audit of the personnel file.

standards.standard_title: Clinical and administrative personnel are orientated to the occupational health service and participate in continuing education, research and other educational experiences to acquire new skills and knowledge and to support job advancement.
standards.intent:
The decision to appoint an individual to a position within the occupational health service sets several processes in motion. To perform well, new personnel need to understand the functioning of the entire occupational health service and how his or her specific role and responsibilities contribute to the service's mission. This is accomplished through a general orientation to the occupational health service and his or her role in the service and a specific orientation to the responsibilities of his or her position. The orientation process should include for example infection control, confidentiality etc.
The occupational health service supports opportunities for continuing education and training of personnel to ensure they remain up to date with current best practice and to acquire advanced or new skills. These opportunities may be offered by the occupational health service, professional association or through educational programmes in the community. The occupational health service supports such opportunities as appropriate to its mission and resources. Such support may be given through tuition support, scheduled time away from work, recognition for achievement and in other ways.

criteria.criterion_text: There are documented processes for orientation of personnel to the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: This includes general orientation and position-specific orientation as discussed in the standard intent above.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service supports continuing education for its clinical personnel and maintains records of this in personnel files.
criteria.guideline: This refers specifically to professional personnel and the requirements for continued registration with the relevant professional bodies, where applicable. Management must have a clear strategy for assisting professional personnel to maintain their continued registration.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a development strategy for the occupational health service that ensures that the occupational health service manager and administrative personnel receive the training required to fulfil their responsibilities.
criteria.guideline: This training could include billing, medical aid codes and triaging, (which is particularly important when administrative personnel are responsible for the initial assessment of patients in the waiting room.

criteria.criterion_text: Personnel members are informed of opportunities to participate in advanced education, training, research, and other experiences.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during an audit of the personnel files of various categories of personnel or other training records. Personnel members may also be interviewed.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service manager is responsible for the implementation and maintenance of a financial strategy.
standards.intent:
Financial planning and management needs to be conducted by a person who is suitably qualified or skilled and experienced in all matters relating to the finances of the occupational health service. Clinical and managerial personnel both need to be included in planning the financial requirements of the service. They require information relating to the funds available to them for the management of the occupational health service and up-to-date statements of current expenditure.
Sound accounting and auditing practices are implemented to ensure transparency. This is guided by documented policies and procedures. The occupational health service manager ensures that these policies and procedures are implemented.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated person is responsible for the implementation and maintenance of a financial strategy.
criteria.guideline: This requires that an individual in the occupational health service has the officially assigned duties of overseeing and taking responsibility for all aspects of financial management. This may be the occupational health service manager, a financial manager or a shared responsibility between occupational health service manager and a financial officer.

criteria.criterion_text: The designated person is suitably qualified and/or experienced in accounting and financial management.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will automatically be scored compliant where a solo private practitioner manages the occupational health services' finances. Where a financial manager or financial officer is employed, compliance will be assessed against the requirements set out in the position description.

criteria.criterion_text: The responsibilities of this person include ensuring that policies and procedures for all financial functions are implemented.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will automatically be scored compliant where a solo private practitioner manages the occupational health services' finances. Where a financial manager or financial officer is employed, policies and procedures must guide their functions.

standards.standard_title: Budgeting and reporting processes are consistent with statutory requirements and accepted standards.
standards.intent: An approved budget should be available in the management documentation. There should be evidence of the allocation of resources in accordance with the approved budget. This ensures that the occupational health service plans for and is able to meet its financial obligations.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a current budget for the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: For occupational health services that comprise of more than one practitioner there should be an approved budget available in the management documentation. For occupational health services that comprise of a solo practitioner there should be evidence of an occupational health service budget. The budget must demonstrate allocation of resources. The budget will include, for example, salaries, rent, utility costs (water and electricity), consumable stock etc.

criteria.criterion_text: A report is produced at least annually, setting out the financial position to date.
criteria.guideline: This criterion requires that at least the most recent financial report that is prepared annually for submission to the relevant authorities such as Botswana Unified Revenue Service (BURS) must be available during the survey.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a mechanism for establishing the reason for budget variation in either income or expenditure.
criteria.guideline: Evidence should exist in the format in which financial statements are produced. Generally, these statements contain a column/section indicating the variance, i.e. under- or over-expenditure. Evidence showing what is done about this variance (especially over-expenditure), should be available, for example, in the minutes of relevant financial management meetings, personnel meetings or memos.

criteria.criterion_text: Capital investment proposals are subject to unanimous agreement among the partners or are agreed according to a voting system acceptable to all partners in the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided to demonstrate agreement. This criterion will be scored "not applicable" in occupational health services owned by a solo practitioner.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service provides services in line with legally and ethically accepted business and financial standards.
standards.intent: The occupational health service has ethical and legal responsibilities to its employee/clients, personnel and the wider community. The leaders understand these responsibilities as they apply to the business activities of the occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has documented ethical and legal policies and procedures for the financial management of the service that are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Examples of compliance with this criterion can be found in the policies and procedures for billing or referral to partners within the occupational health service (for example partners in a poly clinic).

criteria.criterion_text: Internal and external financial audit systems which meet audit requirements are maintained.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured in accordance with the national requirements such as Botswana Companies Act.

criteria.criterion_text: Annual audited financial statements are produced within the required time frame.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL Compliance will be measured in accordance with the national requirements such as of the Botswana Companies Act.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a capital asset register, which is routinely maintained.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: Assets are insured.
criteria.guideline: This includes the occupational health service assets and where applicable the physical structure.

criteria.criterion_text: All health professionals provide evidence of professional indemnity insurance.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during the personnel file audit.

standards.standard_title: There is an effective system to ensure that equipment and supplies are ordered, stored and distributed.
standards.intent:
A competent person ensures that equipment and supplies are ordered timeously, stored safely and distributed appropriately.
Policies and procedures are developed for the various provisioning functions. Such policies should include as a minimum:
Ordering of and payment for supplies and equipment
Safe storage of supplies
Condemning procedures
Security of order books, prescription pads and other face-value documents
The occupational health service needs to ensure that appropriate control measures are in place and finances are made available for the purchase of those items of equipment and supplies which have been identified as being required by clinical and managerial personnel.

criteria.criterion_text: An individual is designated to control the ordering, storage, distribution and control of equipment and supplies used in the occupational health service.
Policies and procedures relating to all aspects of provisioning/supply chain management are implemented.
criteria.guideline: As a minimum, the policies and procedures discussed in a) – d) in the standard intent above should be available, accessible to personnel and implementation should be monitored.

criteria.criterion_text: Secure storage facilities are available.
criteria.guideline: The store must be lockable and exclude any unauthorised entry.

criteria.criterion_text: Prescription pads, letterheads, investigation request forms, administrative records and other official documents are accessible only to authorised persons.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by observation and personnel interviews.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service manager and personnel work collaboratively to develop, implement and maintain effective risk management systems in the service.
standards.intent:
To plan effectively, the occupational health service must be aware of all relevant risks. The goal is to prevent accidents and injuries, maintain safe and secure conditions for employee/clients, families, personnel and reduce and control hazards and risks. A risk management programme includes:
Comprehensive risk assessment of the occupational health service
Designing all aspects of the risk management plan (financial, physical, environmental, medicolegal, operational etc.)
Implementation of the programme
Personnel education
Testing and monitoring the programme
Periodic review and revision of the programme
Monitoring of all aspects of the programme provides valuable data to make improvements in the programme and further reduce risks within the occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: There are documented risk management processes for identifying all risks relating to the occupational health service processes and systems, personnel, employee/clients, visitors to the service and physical facilities.
criteria.guideline: Compliance with this criterion requires documented evidence that the occupational health service personnel have collaboratively identified risks associated with occupational health and safety. This document will be used to develop an action plan to eliminate or minimise risk. The risk management process should include all relevant aspects of the occupational health service, for example employee/client, personnel and visitor related risks; financial, corporate and legal risks; physical facility, security and environmental risks; etc. This does not necessarily require a single integrated document, provided all components are dealt with in documented systems for the relevant operational processes/functions/sections. (NOTE: doing only monthly workplace inspections does not qualify for a compliance rating).

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service manager ensures the development and implementation of written policies and procedures for risk management processes and activities.
criteria.guideline: These policies and procedures must define the routine risk management processes, for example, monthly inspections to monitor risks, and the actions to be taken when risks materialise as adverse events. The policies and procedures should include the arrangements in place to manage the following risks:  • Slips and trips Manual handling Violence and aggression Work related stress Hazardous substances The risks to new and expectant mothers at work

criteria.criterion_text: A nominated individual with relevant qualifications, skills and/or experience supervises the implementation of the risk management programme.
criteria.guideline: In small occupational health services, this will usually be the occupational health service manager. Larger occupational health services may have a designated risk manager.

criteria.criterion_text: Ongoing in-service training of all personnel in risk management policies, procedures and principles is documented.
criteria.guideline: A record must be kept of such training and must include all personnel.

criteria.criterion_text: Risk management systems are reviewed whenever there are changes in occupational health service systems and processes, or physical facilities.
criteria.guideline: This will include for example, during building renovations, changing from paper-based to electronic employee/client records or financial documentation, changing service providers etc.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service designs and implements a coordinated programme to reduce the risk of infection in employees/clients and healthcare workers.
standards.intent:
For an infection prevention and control programme to be effective, it must be comprehensive, encompassing both clinical care and employee/client health. The programme is appropriate to the size and geographic location of the occupational health service, the services offered, and the employee/client seen by the service.
Infections can enter the occupational health service via employees/clients, their families, personnel, visitors, other individuals and vectors. All areas of the occupational health service where these individuals or vectors are found must therefore be included in the programme of infection surveillance, prevention and control. Certain infections require employees/clients suffering from these infections to be separated from non-infected employees/clients such as employees/clients with TB or high-risk influenza. These employees/clients are identified when requesting an appointment and appropriately triaged.
The programme is managed by a nominated individual within the occupational health service and all personnel are informed of the nomination. Their qualifications depend on the activities they will carry out and may be met through education, training and experience. Coordination involves communication with all parts of the service to ensure that the programme is continuous and proactive.
Whatever the mechanism chosen by the service to coordinate the infection control programme, medical and nursing personnel are represented and engaged in the activities. The individual, committee, or other mechanism must also monitor those support services which may lead to the spread of infection, for example, cleaning and waste disposal.
Hand washing and disinfecting agents are fundamental to infection prevention and control. Soap and disinfectants are located in those areas where hand washing and disinfecting procedures are required.
Personnel are educated in proper hand washing and disinfecting procedures.

criteria.criterion_text: A nominated individual is responsible for infection control in the occupational health service and personnel are aware of the nomination.
criteria.guideline: It is this nominated individual's responsibility to ensure that infection control policies and procedures are implemented and monitored.

criteria.criterion_text: Written policies and procedures guide personnel in the implementation of the infection control programme.
criteria.guideline: These policies and procedures must be available and accessible in the occupational health service and their implementation monitored.

criteria.criterion_text: Regular in-service training is given to all personnel in the field of infection control and is documented.
criteria.guideline: The infection control education programme needs to include policies/guidelines as well as relevant issues as they are identified. All personnel should be included in information sharing and training at regular scheduled meetings or other fora. Documented evidence (for example, minutes of meetings/attendance records) must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Infection control is on the agenda of all personnel meetings of the occupational health service and discussion points are documented.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: All employee/client and personnel areas of the occupational health service are included in the documented infection control programme.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  This includes for example, all clinical areas, the waiting area, personnel and employee/client toilets, kitchen etc.

criteria.criterion_text: Hand washing and disinfecting facilities, including warm water, soap, paper towels or hand sanitisers are available in all relevant areas.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Personnel are constantly reminded of the importance of effective hand washing and promote health and hygiene aimed at the prevention of occupational conditions that lead to communicable diseases.
criteria.guideline: Posters are displayed at basins, hand hygiene audits are regularly conducted etc.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service reports on notifiable diseases to appropriate external public health agencies promptly and ensures the referral and uptake of confirmed communicable diseases into appropriate treatment programmes.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: A nominated individual has been trained in and is responsible for sterilisation procedures within the occupational health service and can describe the process in detail.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of training is required. This criterion is only applicable to those occupational health services that have on-site sterilisers.

criteria.criterion_text: Relevant personnel members are immunised against Hepatitis B according to occupational health service policy.
criteria.guideline: This requires the occupational health service to formally identify the categories of personnel who are at risk (including cleaning personnel if they handle healthcare related waste) and to ensure that there is a system to track that their Hepatitis B immunisations remain up to date.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented policy for the management of exposure to high risk infections and needle-stick injuries.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided to demonstrate that personnel are aware of the policy and have been trained on its content and implementation. Compliance will further be verified by personnel interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Post exposure prophylaxis is available to personnel in accordance with national policy and includes the management of the employee/client from whom the needle was withdrawn.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  This includes exposure to high risk infections and needle-stick injuries (for example body fluid splashes etc.) The national policy must be available and known to personnel.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented policy for the management of body fluid spills.
criteria.guideline: The policy must include as a minimum: The correct cleaning procedure following a body fluid spill The correct procedure to follow when sluicing/washing contaminated linen

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented policy for the triage of employee/clients with potential communicable diseases.
criteria.guideline: These employees/clients are identified when requesting an appointment and appropriately triaged. If it is necessary for these employees/clients to attend the occupational health service, they are directed to a separate waiting area to prevent transmission of the infection to non-infected employees/clients. Where there is no separate waiting area, the practitioner must attend to the employees/clients immediately.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service identifies, investigates and monitors the outbreak of communicable disease when indicated.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service institutes the necessary corrective and preventive occupational health measures relevant to the employee’s/client’s workplace when required.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has a written policy which considers the need for infection control procedures relating to the handling, storing and disposing of waste.
standards.intent:
Policies need to be developed to guide personnel in ensuring their own safety, the safety of others and the safety of the environment when implementing waste removal systems.
Household waste, hazardous wastes (such as chemicals and hazardous gases), pharmaceutical and healthcare waste are identified by the occupational health service and are safely controlled in accordance with a written policy. All healthcare waste is regarded as hazardous or potentially hazardous. The policy is included in the occupational health service's risk management plan.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has a waste management policy that is consistent with country specific laws and regulations and includes the safe handling, storing and disposing of all different types of waste.
criteria.guideline: The policy must include all relevant aspects of waste management, for example, identification, colour coding, handling, storage, disposal of etc. with special reference to clinical/healthcare waste, personal protective equipment (PPE), the management of spills and the reporting and investigation of waste related incidents.

criteria.criterion_text: The policy makes provision for the appropriate management of confidential waste.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Confidential waste includes all employee/client and personnel related documentation and occupational health service specific documentation for example, financial statements.

criteria.criterion_text: Waste is segregated in accordance with policies, procedures, country-specific laws and regulations.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: The colour of bag and type of container appropriate to the type of waste generated are available.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Waste is protected from theft, vandalism or scavenging by animals.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Waste is collected at appropriate times so that hazards are not caused.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation and by documented evidence that demonstrates when and by whom waste is collected.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service makes provision for the safety and security of personnel, visitors, employee/client and facilities.
standards.intent: Consideration is given to the safety and security of personnel, visitors, employee/ client and facilities during working hours and after hours. Plans are developed and implemented to provide protection from attack, theft or damage to the property.

criteria.criterion_text: Security systems, including guards if required, provide for internal and external security.
criteria.guideline: If the security system is outsourced (alarms/guards) the service contract must be available during the survey. As a minimum, an occupational health service must be secured with burglar bars and security gates. Compliance will be further measured by observation of the security measures in place and whether they are appropriate for the individual setting.

criteria.criterion_text: Sufficient light sources are available to provide adequate light (no dark areas) in all areas such as the entrance, waiting rooms, halls and offices.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: There is effective control of access to clinical areas and store areas.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Alarm systems and signals are tested every month.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will only be applicable to those occupational health services that have alarm systems and signals (for example panic buttons) installed. Documented evidence of testing must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: A mechanism known to the personnel is available for summoning the assistance of security/police/protection services in the case of an emergency.
criteria.guideline: This can be achieved for example, by including the information in the orientation programme for new personnel and by displaying emergency numbers next to all telephones in the occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: Reasonable measures are taken to ensure the safety of lone workers.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service implements structured systems to ensure fire safety.
standards.intent:
Fire is an ever-present risk in an occupational health service. As such, the service needs to plan for:
•   The prevention of fires through the reduction of risks, such as the safe storage and handling of potentially flammable materials
Safe and unobstructed means of exit in the event of fire
Clearly depicted fire escape routes
Inspection reports from the local fire departments
Suppression mechanisms such as water hoses, chemical suppressants or sprinkler systems
These actions when combined, give employees/clients, families, personnel and visitors adequate time to exit the facility safely in the event of a fire or smoke. These actions must be effective irrespective of the age, size or construction of the facility.
The fire safety plan for the occupational health service includes:
The frequency of inspection, testing and maintenance of fire protection and safety systems, consistent with requirements
The necessary education of personnel to protect and evacuate client/worker/employee/client effectively in the event of fire or smoke
The process for testing the plan for the safe evacuation of the facility in the event of a fire or smoke
A mock evacuation to be carried out at least once a year
The required documentation of all inspection, testing and maintenance systems
The occupational health service develops and implements a policy and plan to eliminate smoking in the facility or to limit smoking to designated non-employee/client care areas.

criteria.criterion_text: There are structured systems and processes in place to ensure that all occupants of the occupational health service's facilities are safe from fire or smoke.
criteria.guideline: There are documented fire safety systems which include all the relevant aspects of fire safety, for example training, rehearsals, servicing and storage of equipment, escape route signage, storage and handling of flammable materials, etc.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented certification is available from the relevant authority to show that the facility complies with applicable laws and regulations in relation to fire safety (fire clearance certificate).
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  As per the intent statement, compliance with this criterion requires documented evidence of an official inspection to confirm that the building complies with national fire safety requirements. The occupational health service manager must obtain a copy of the documentation from the landlord if the service does not own the building.

criteria.criterion_text: Firefighting equipment is regularly inspected and serviced at least annually, and the date of the service is recorded on the apparatus.
criteria.guideline: Abatement systems as per country specific requirements will be reflected in the certification as required in1.7.5.2. It is essential that the testing and servicing of all fire safety equipment is up to date, automatic abatement systems are regularly tested, fire and smoke detection systems are tested, and automatic abatement doors are not forced to remain open by means of wedging or putting objects against them. Documented evidence of inspection, testing and maintenance of fire safety equipment is required. This may include the fixing of service labels onto the equipment itself.

criteria.criterion_text: Flammable materials are clearly labelled and safely stored.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL Flammable materials are identified by the occupational health service and stored in accordance with the national safety regulations. The storage precautions are applicable to all areas/services/departments where flammable materials are used. The appropriateness of the storage facility will be determined by the quantity and flashpoint of the materials stored. Bulk storage requirements and registration will be determined by country specific regulations.

criteria.criterion_text: Sufficient electrical socket outlets are provided in all areas to avoid overloading of individual outlets and to minimise fire risks.
Easily recognised and understood signs prohibiting smoking are displayed in areas where flammable materials and combustible gases are stored.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the assessment of the areas where such materials are stored.

criteria.criterion_text: A floor plan showing the location of firefighting equipment, electrical distribution board, evacuation routes and emergency exits is displayed.
criteria.guideline: The display of such plans throughout the occupational health service will be observed during the visit to each area.

criteria.criterion_text: Annual personnel training in fire prevention and evacuation procedures is documented.
criteria.guideline: A fire safety evacuation plan must be developed by the occupational health service. Personnel training must be provided, and evacuation exercises held annually. Documented evidence that the plan is physically rehearsed will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: A mechanism known to personnel is available for summoning the fire service.
criteria.guideline: This can be achieved for example, by including the information in the orientation programme for new personnel and by displaying emergency numbers next to all telephones in the occupational health service.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service develops a written plan to respond to emergencies.
standards.intent:
Personnel may be affected by community emergencies, epidemics and major events such as damage caused by a natural disasters and this in turn may affect the functioning of the practice. Practices should also be prepared for bomb threats, flooding, natural disasters, explosions and the consequent loss of vital services, failure of water and electrical supplies and hostage taking.
There may be a time when it is necessary to evacuate employees/clients, visitors and personnel. This can only be done quickly and effectively if personnel are trained in evacuation procedures.
The emergency plan for the occupational health service includes:
The necessary education of personnel to protect and evacuate employees/clients effectively when an emergency occurs
The need for each personnel member to participate in at least one emergency preparedness test per year

criteria.criterion_text: There is a written plan to deal with emergencies (including bomb threats, fire, flooding, natural disasters, failure of water and electrical supplies).
criteria.guideline: The documented plan is up to date and is available in all areas of the occupational health service

criteria.criterion_text: There are site and floor plans that depict the locations and layout of the main services (for example, water, sanitation, electricity supply).
criteria.guideline: It is very important that all personnel know where to switch off the main services, for example, water, electricity and, where installed, oxygen supply. Compliance will be measured on documented evidence and personnel interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented evidence is available to show that the personnel participate in a rehearsal of the emergency plan at least annually.
criteria.guideline: The emphasis of this rehearsal must be on the emergency situations mentioned in the standard intent. Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: First aid kits and materials for healthcare workers are available.
criteria.guideline: Botswana specific occupational health laws and regulations should dictate the contents of the first aid kits. Compliance will be measured on the availability of the required stock

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has a system to ensure that data and information is made available to meet user needs and the needs of those outside the service who require data and information from the occupational health service.
standards.intent:
To provide coordinated and integrated services, occupational health services rely on information relating to individual employees/clients, care provided, results of care and their own performance.
Every occupational health service seeks to obtain, manage and use information to improve employee/client outcomes as well as individual and overall occupational health service performance. The information management process makes it possible to combine information from various sources and generate reports to support decision making. The combination of clinical and managerial information supports the leaders of the occupational health service to plan collaboratively.
Planning incorporates input from a variety of sources:
The care providers
The administration team
The occupational health service managers
Those inside and outside the occupational health service who require information about the service's operational and care processes
Those individuals in the occupational health service who generate, collect, analyse and use the information are educated and trained to participate effectively in the management of information and to understand the need for security and confidentiality of this information.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented policy for collection, collation, validation and distribution of data which is implemented.
criteria.guideline: The policy must define those permitted access to each category of data and information.

criteria.criterion_text: Clinical, managerial and administrative personnel participate in developing and implementing an information system to support employee/client care and occupational health service management.
criteria.guideline: This does not have to be a single system, nor does it have to be electronic. Paper-based information system/s are also acceptable. The information required by the criteria must encompass at least information relating to individual employees/clients, care provided, results of care and the occupational health service’s own performance

criteria.criterion_text: Clinical and managerial data and information are integrated as needed to support decision-making.
criteria.guideline: Assessment of compliance is based on the availability of reports on the various types of data as well as documented evidence (for example, minutes of meetings or other form of communication) of discussions on the information and how this is taken into consideration to assist with decision-making processes. This needs to happen at all levels of the occupational health service and not just at management level, as information also needs to be available on departmental operations such as personnel matters, financial aspects, supply management, outcomes of quality improvement programmes, results from clinical and employee/client record audits, negative incidents, etc.

criteria.criterion_text: Required technology and other resources support the implementation of the data and information management system.
criteria.guideline: Resource requirements may differ vastly between paper-based and computerised systems and the assessment of compliance needs to take these factors into account.

criteria.criterion_text: Security and confidentiality of data and information is maintained.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL Security relates to access control in terms of passwords, back up processes as well as archiving of paper-based records. Up to date anti-virus/anti-malware software is in place. Confidentiality aspects need to be documented as part of the information management processes for the various types of information, whether it be financial data, personal information on personnel, employee/client information, etc. Where applicable, country-specific legal requirements need to be considered.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented procedures which outline the processes to provide required information to individuals and agencies outside the occupational health service when required by laws or regulations are implemented.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Documented evidence of reporting is required. This includes notifiable disease reporting, other statistics required according to country-specific legislation and information provided to healthcare funders.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service manager or delegated person checks the integrity of data leaving the facility for completeness, correctness and consistency.
criteria.guideline: The integrity of data refers to the validation of raw data to ensure accuracy of information. Evidence of quality checks of the data before it leaves the occupational health service and action taken when deficiencies in data quality and timeframes of reporting are identified. This includes for example, ICD 10 codes supplied to healthcare funders, national statistics etc.

standards.standard_title: There is a system for the analysis of data.
standards.intent:
To reach conclusions and make decisions, data must be aggregated, analysed and transformed into useful information. Data analysis is done by individuals with an understanding of information management who also have skills in data aggregation methods and in the use of various statistical tools. To maximise effectiveness, data analysis involves the individuals responsible for the process or outcome being measured. These individuals may be clinical, managerial, administrative or a combination. When implemented in this way, data analysis provides continuous feedback of quality management information to help those individuals make decisions and continuously improve the process under review.
The occupational health service determines how often data is aggregated and analysed. The frequency depends on the activity or area being measured, the frequency of measurement, and the service's priorities. For example, clinical data may be analysed once or twice yearly to monitor care in chronic disease management and the performance of contracted services may be analysed quarterly to ensure on-going adequacy of service provision. Aggregation of data at points in time enables the occupational health service to judge a process's stability or an outcome's predictability in relation to expectations. Computers are a useful tool in this process.
The goal of data analysis is to be able to compare an occupational health service in four ways:
With itself over time
With other similar health facilities
With standards
With evidence-based practice and guidelines
These comparisons help the occupational health service to understand the source and nature of undesirable change and help to focus improvement efforts.
Understanding statistical techniques is helpful in data analysis, especially in interpreting variation and in deciding where improvement is needed. Run charts, control charts, histograms and Pareto charts are examples of statistical tools useful in understanding trends and variations in health care.

criteria.criterion_text: Data is aggregated, analysed and transformed into useful, relevant information for monitoring and improving the service.
criteria.guideline: The information generated by data collection tools, for example infection control audit tools, employee/client satisfaction surveys etc. must be analysed. This criterion will be scored non-compliant where there is only raw data available.

criteria.criterion_text: The frequency of data collection and analysis is appropriate to the process under study.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured on documented evidence that the frequency of data collection is established for each process under study.

criteria.criterion_text: Statistical tools and techniques are used in the analysis process when suitable.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required for compliance.

criteria.criterion_text: Information relating to the quality of the services delivered by the occupational health service is made available to employees/clients who use the service and other relevant parties.
criteria.guideline: This can be achieved by displaying the results in the waiting area, on personnel notice boards, on the occupational health service website etc. The method of information sharing and the parties with whom it is shared will depend on the nature of the information.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service appoints an individual or committee which represents all services within the practice to guide the quality improvement process.
standards.intent:
Leadership and planning are essential if an occupational health service is to initiate and maintain improvement. All leaders participate in establishing the service's commitment and approach to improvement as well as programme management and supervision.
Improvement programmes are most effective when they are planned service-wide. The framework for these is provided in a written plan for the programme, which is inclusive of all services within the occupational health service and of all related quality activities such as infection control and risk management activities.
The quality improvement process must:
Be consistent with the occupational health service's mission and strategic plans
Meet the needs of employees/clients, families, personnel and other healthcare team members
Use current, clinical practice guidelines and other relevant evidence-based information
Include sound business practices
Incorporate relevant risk management information
Occupational health service managers and personnel prioritise those critical, high risk, high cost, high volume or problem-prone processes that are most directly related to the quality of care and the safety of the environment. Available data and information are used to identify priority areas.
Participation in data collection, analysis, planning and implementation of quality improvement programmes require knowledge and skills. Personnel receive training consistent with their role in the planned activity. The occupational health service identifies or provides a knowledgeable trainer for this education. Personnel are permitted to attend training as part of their assigned responsibilities. Managerial and clinical personnel participate in the process.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated individual oversees the information and quality management and improvement processes.
criteria.guideline: This requires that an individual has the assigned duty of overseeing and taking responsibility for all aspects of information and quality management and improvement processes. The training and experience required will depend on the level of care provided by the occupational health service and the complexity of the information systems for which the individual is responsible. In some occupational health services this may be the practice manager or the solo practitioner, in other occupational health services a quality manager may be appointed.

criteria.criterion_text: All occupational health service personnel are informed about the function of the information and quality management individual.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during personnel interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: There are formal systems and processes for quality management and improvement.
criteria.guideline: This includes all aspects of the occupational health service. Such processes may exist in different formats, for example, a detailed single document, different documented systems or electronic modules. Whatever format exists, the intention is that the “processes” should be comprehensive and reflect all components of quality management and improvement in the service.

criteria.criterion_text: Personnel are trained in the implementation of quality management processes.

The occupational health service manager provides technology, support and resources (including time) for the assessment and improvement of the service’s management, clinical and support processes and this is reflected in the strategic plan/business plan for the service.
criteria.guideline: The technology, support and resources required will be determined by the design and complexity of the quality management and improvement framework. This may include aspects such as training in quality methodology, aligning work schedules to allow for time to spend on relevant activities, providing assistance with data management (manually or electronically), etc.

criteria.criterion_text: The leaders set priorities for improvement activities based on high risk, high cost and/or high volume or problem-prone areas.
criteria.guideline: As part of the occupational health service’s quality management and improvement framework, the leaders should play an active role in collaborating with teams and individuals in the service to decide which activities to include in the formal monitoring for quality improvement purposes.

criteria.criterion_text: Each function within the occupational health service implements relevant quality improvement activities.
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service quality management and improvement framework needs to guide personnel on how to establish quality improvement processes and initiatives with regard to the development of relevant standards for their service, for example, administration, housekeeping, clinical care etc., the development of measurable indicators for monitoring purposes, and the implementation of remedial actions.

criteria.criterion_text: The objectives, scope, implementation and effectiveness of the activities to assess and improve quality are evaluated regularly and revised as necessary.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided to demonstrate that the quality management and improvement programme is evaluated and revised as necessary.

standards.standard_title: Key monitoring, measurement and evaluation processes are planned and implemented.
standards.intent:
A comprehensive approach to quality management and improvement includes the following processes:
Planning for improvement in quality
Monitoring developments regarding best practice and implementing these as appropriate
Monitoring processes through indicator data collection
Analysing the data
Implementing and sustaining changes that result in improvement
These processes provide the framework for the occupational health service team to achieve ongoing quality improvement thereby assuring their employees/clients of quality care, reflective of current best practice in the rapidly developing world of health care.
The monitoring of clinical and management functions results in the accumulation of data and information. An understanding of how well the occupational health service is doing rests on repeated analysis of the data, information over time and comparison with other occupational health services. The leaders of an occupational health service make the selection of key measures to be included in the monitoring activities.

criteria.criterion_text: Targets (goals) are set for the desired levels of employee/client care and occupational health service management.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service collects data relevant to each identified indicator in the quality improvement programme for the monitoring and improvement of employee/client care and occupational health service management.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: As part of clinical monitoring, structured clinical audits are done to monitor the implementation of clinical guidelines.

standards.standard_title: Analysed data is used to improve the quality of managerial and clinical services.
standards.intent:
Personnel selected to participate in the management and supervision of improvement programmes are those closest to the activities or processes being monitored, studied or improved.
When negative incidents or adverse events occur, the occupational health service and its leaders evaluate the processes that led to the error or event. Faulty processes are redesigned, tested and monitored to ensure that the same or similar errors or events do not occur again.
Case reviews are performed for all new diagnoses of significant life threatening diseases, unexpected deaths and management of emergency cases that present at the occupational health service. The routine review of these cases assists in the identification of what went well and what could have been done better to inform continuous improvement in clinical care and enable sharing of best practice.
When the occupational health service detects or suspects an undesirable change from what is expected, it initiates intense analysis to determine where best to focus improvement. In particular, intense analysis is initiated when levels, patterns or trends vary significantly or undesirably from:
•   What is expected
Those of other occupational health services
Recognised standards
Each occupational health service establishes which events are significant and the process for their intense analysis. When undesirable events can be prevented, the occupational health service works to carry out preventive changes.

criteria.criterion_text: Information from the findings of quality assessment and improvement activities is used to detect trends, patterns and opportunities to improve or prevent potential problems.
criteria.guideline: This requires evidence that areas for improvement are identified from all the data collected during occupational health service activities.

criteria.criterion_text: When appropriate, an improvement plan is developed in collaboration with all relevant team members and an implementation process and acceptable timeframe is agreed to by the team and implemented.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: A time for repeat data collection and analysis is agreed and completed and the results discussed by the relevant team members.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service holds regular meetings to discuss significant clinical issues.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of significant clinical issues can be determined from the incident management processes and during employee/client record audits and personnel interviews. Minutes of meetings and attendance records must be provided as evidence of compliance with this criterion.

criteria.criterion_text: Information from a validated employee/client satisfaction audit tool is used to improve the quality of service delivery.
criteria.guideline: This requires that the occupational health service does more than just provide information about the complaints process. Formal employee/client/family satisfaction surveys must be conducted regularly using an audit tool that is specifically designed to elicit information about the occupational health service and the services that it offers. The tools used to measure employee/client feedback need to be rigorous and include all areas of the occupational health service (for example waiting times, attitude of personnel, cleanliness of toilets, communication with clinical personnel, education etc.) to ensure the integrity of data subsequently used by service for quality improvement purposes. The occupational health service must decide on the method, frequency and number of employees/clients to be surveyed but compliance with the criterion requires that it is representative of the size of the occupational health service and the services offered. The occupational health service needs to further provide documented evidence that the information from the survey was used to improve the quality of service delivery.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has a documented policy for formal review of adverse events within the service.
standards.intent:
As a minimum, the occupational health service should have a system for recording, analysing, discussing and learning from adverse events within the service. This should include clinical, managerial, administrative and all other adverse events. The data collected, analysis of the data, discussions surrounding the event, decisions based on the discussions and any suggested changes should be documented and retained. A nominated personnel member must be responsible for this process and for the implementation, monitoring and review of the changes. This ensures that the occupational health service learns from its mistakes and prevents recurrence of the same mistakes, thereby providing continuous improvement in service delivery. Lessons learned could be shared with other occupational health services to provide benchmarking.
Clinically significant events such as medication errors, for example prescribing a drug to an employee/client when the records indicate that the employee/client is allergic to the drug or employee/client identification errors for example, performing a laboratory or radiological test on the wrong employee/client should always precipitate intense analysis to understand the cause and prevent recurrence.
All records relating to these discussions should be anonymised.

criteria.criterion_text: A documented procedure for the monitoring of near misses/incidents/adverse (sentinel) events is available, which includes the documentation of interventions, responses and corrective actions taken to recorded incidents.
criteria.guideline: A policy and procedure should be available to all personnel detailing the steps to follow in the event of such an incident occurring. This includes the reporting, recording and investigation of the event as well as the response to the event and the actions taken to prevent recurrence of the event, or minimise harm should the event recur. Personnel will be interviewed to confirm that they are familiar with the contents of these documents.

criteria.criterion_text: Formal significant event analyses are undertaken when necessary.
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service manager/risk manager must identify those events where formal event analyses must be conducted as required by law, regulation and occupational health service policy. All events that meet the definition must be assessed by performing a credible root cause analysis.
