When the root cause analysis reveals that systems improvement or other actions can prevent or reduce the risk of such events recurring, appropriate action must be taken to achieve this risk reduction.

criteria.criterion_text: Notes are kept regarding the data analysis and actions arising from formal incident reviews.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required for compliance.

criteria.criterion_text: All changes to policy and procedure that are identified as a result of these formal incident reviews are documented and included in new policies or incorporated into existing policies.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required for compliance.

criteria.criterion_text: The implementation of these new policies/procedures is delegated to a nominated individual who is responsible for monitoring the effectiveness of the changes and arranging reviews if appropriate.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required for compliance.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service regularly assesses the quality and the completeness of the employee/client record content.
standards.intent:
The clinical record of each employee/client needs to contain sufficient information to support the diagnosis, justify the treatment provided and document the care given. Where carry cards are used, there are summaries of each attendance in the service which will provide this information. A standardised format and content of employee’s/client's records will help promote the integration and continuity of care among the various providers of care to the employee/client. The occupational health service determines the specific data and information recorded in the clinical record. Each service has a process to assess the quality and completeness of employee/client records. This is a part of the performance improvement activities of the occupational health service and is carried out regularly. This information is used to improve the quality of clinical record keeping.
Clinical record review is based on a representative sample of the practitioners providing care and of the types of care provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/client records are reviewed regularly, and results analysed as part of the quality improvement process.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of such audits must be provided. Evaluation of results and remedial action taken must be documented as well as maintenance of achieved improvements over time.

criteria.criterion_text: The review uses a representative sample.
criteria.guideline: A representative sample is a sufficiently large sample which includes records for all practitioners providing employee/client care and all types of care provided. The review should be conducted by clinical professionals authorised to make entries in the employee/client record.

criteria.criterion_text: Records comply with professionally acceptable norms (including legal requirements where applicable) relating to signature, use of abbreviations and legibility.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the client/worker/employee record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Standardised diagnosis and procedure codes are used.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed by comparing the codes used in the occupational health service with country specific directives. The responsible department should therefore have both the directives and the occupational health service's coding system available for comparison.

criteria.criterion_text: Symbols and definitions are standardised.
criteria.guideline: The use of abbreviations provides an opportunity for miscommunication and confusion which can result in employee/client harm. Best practice is therefore not to use them at all. However, where they are permitted, their use must be standardised, and all relevant personnel must be made aware of the meaning of each abbreviation.

criteria.criterion_text: FACILITIES, EQUIPMENT AND CONTRACTED SERVICES
OVERVIEW OF  FACILITIES, EQUIPMENT AND CONTRACTED SERVICES

Laws, regulations and inspections by national governmental and local authorities determine in large part how a facility is designed, used and maintained. All occupational health services, regardless of their size and resources, must comply with these requirements as part of their responsibilities to employees/clients, families and personnel. Occupational health services begin by complying with relevant laws and regulations. Over time they become more knowledgeable about the details of the physical facility they occupy. They begin to gather data proactively and carry out strategies to reduce risks and enhance the employee/client care environment.
Buildings, grounds, utilities and equipment are maintained and do not pose hazards to the occupants. The personnel providing the maintenance service are knowledgeable and competent.

Occupational health services need to have the necessary equipment for comprehensive primary care and emergency resuscitation. To meet the standards, equipment must be in good working order. There is a wide range of equipment that occupational health services may need in order to provide services which meet national and local needs, serve the nature of the occupational health service and support any procedures that the occupational health service performs.

Where service contracts/agreements are awarded to outside agencies, the occupational health service must ensure that there is a written contractual agreement outlining the service and standard of service to be delivered. Contracted agencies must undertake to provide services in accordance with infection control and health and safety requirements. Where applicable, the contracted personnel receive training with regard to waste disposal and infection control if this has not been undertaken to a satisfactory level by the contracted company.
Standards

standards.standard_title: Measures are in place to ensure that employee/client access to the occupational health service is facilitated by adequate infrastructural arrangements.

criteria.criterion_text: Directional signs to the occupational health service are clearly readable and up to date.
criteria.guideline: This requirement ensures that members of the public, both locals and visitors, who are not familiar with the location of the occupational health service will be able to find it when necessary.

criteria.criterion_text: A telephone/emergency number is available and provided to employees/clients on registration and on request.
criteria.guideline: This can be in the form of a business card or employee/client information leaflet. Where the occupational health service does not provide emergency services the emergency number must reflect the closest emergency facility to the occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: A phone number for the after-hours service of the occupational health service is clearly displayed on the outside of the facility.
criteria.guideline: Where the occupational health service does not offer an after-hours service, an emergency number must be displayed. Compliance will be verified by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Parking is provided close to the building entrance for employees/clients, including the physically challenged.
criteria.guideline: Surveyors will make a judgement based on the size of the occupational health service and services provided.

criteria.criterion_text: There is wheelchair access to and within the building.
criteria.guideline: This will be assessed during the visit to each occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: Ramps and stairs include safety features such as rails.
criteria.guideline: The provision of such safety features will be assessed during the visit to each occupational health service.

standards.standard_title: Functional facilities are available to provide safety and comfort for employees/clients, personnel and other visitors.
standards.intent:
In order to provide safe employee/client care, each unit requires adequate resources. The building is appropriate for an occupational health service in terms of size and layout.
The physical facilities required include adequate office accommodation for personnel, a designated sluice area which is hygienically clean at all times, treatment and dressing rooms. Cleaning equipment is safely stored in a room or cupboard used for this purpose only. There are adequate toilet facilities for the number of clients and personnel as determined by country-specific legislation. There is adequate lighting and ventilation.
Each consulting room, which may include an attached examination room/area:
Is free from excessive noise
Has adequate lighting
Is maintained at a comfortable ambient temperature
Ensures employee/client privacy when the employee/client needs to undress for a clinical examination (for example the use of adequate curtains or screens and gowns or sheets)
Buildings and grounds are maintained and do not pose hazards to the occupants. The construction of the building in terms of walls, ceilings, floors, doors and windows must be sound. The general appearance will be examined for neatness, condition of paintwork, signs of leakage, mould spots etc.

criteria.criterion_text: Laws, regulations and other requirements applicable to the occupational health service's facilities are implemented and are available to the personnel.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  A copy of the occupation certificate issued in terms of the Building Control Act and regulations must be available during the survey. Where the practice is in a rented facility, a copy must be obtained from the landlord.  Copies of the laws, regulations and other requirements applicable to the structure of the facility should be available and accessible to personnel in either hard copy or electronic format and should include at least the Building Control Act and regulations

criteria.criterion_text: The building is appropriate as a healthcare facility in terms of size and lay-out.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: The lay-out of the facility allows for effective flow of employee/client care.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: The waiting area is sufficient to accommodate the usual number of employees/clients and other people who could be waiting at any given time.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation and by examination of daily employee/client attendance figures.

criteria.criterion_text: The waiting area caters for the specific safety needs of children, where applicable.
criteria.guideline: This includes for example, no open electrical sockets, measures to prevent children leaving the occupational health service unnoticed, measures to prevent injury from inappropriate toys (ingestion of small objects, sharp protrusions, easily cleanable), measures to secure hazardous substances etc.

criteria.criterion_text: There is at least one consulting/examination room for every member of the clinical team working in the occupational health service at any time.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: All areas of the facility, including consultation rooms are clean, well ventilated and well maintained.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Sufficient office/administrative space is available for the personnel.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Toilet/washroom facilities are clean and in working order.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Separate sanitary facilities are provided for personnel.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a separate, secure area for personnel with adequate secure storage facilities for outdoor clothing, handbags and personal possessions.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Required furniture is available according to established lists and functioning properly.
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service must compile and maintain an inventory of furniture which must be available at the survey. As a minimum, an occupational health service must have at least the following: Chairs (in the waiting area, for administrative personnel, for clinical personnel and for employees/clients and families in the treatment areas) Desks/workstations (sufficient for the size of the occupational health service) Examination bed (in each examination area) Lockable fire proof file cabinets Lockable fire proof cabinet (for all confidential documentation) Refuse bins for domestic and clinical waste in each functional area where appropriate.

criteria.criterion_text: Hand washing facilities, including warm water, soap and paper towels are available for employees/clients and personnel.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Laboratory services are available to meet the needs of employees/clients.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: A register is kept of all laboratory specimens sent and results received.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Radiology services are available to meet the needs of employees/clients.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

standards.standard_title: Medical equipment is available and properly maintained to meet the needs of the employee/client population.
standards.intent:
Occupational health services are responsible for ensuring that appropriate medical equipment is available and ready for use always. There is an accountable, systematic approach to ensuring that cost-effective, safe and appropriate medical equipment is available to meet the demands of quality employee/client care.
The occupational health service manager takes responsibility for ensuring that medical equipment is available, appropriately maintained and calibrated and that the relevant personnel are competent to use it.
Each occupational health service ensures that it has the required equipment for comprehensive primary care detailed as a minimum below.
Auroscope
Blood glucose monitoring equipment
Diagnostic set
Disposable syringes and needles
Eye examination equipment (for example, fluorescein staining, ophthalmoscope)
Examination light
Gloves (sterile and non-sterile)
Height measurement device
Measuring tape
Monofilament for sensation testing
Nebulising equipment and supplies
Oxygen
Patella hammer
Peak flow meter
Scales
Spacer for inhaler
Specimen collection equipment
Sphygmomanometer with small medium and large cuffs
Stethoscope
Surgical masks
Thermometer
Torch
Tourniquet
Urine testing strips
Visual acuity charts

criteria.criterion_text: Implemented policies and procedures guide the management of medical equipment.
criteria.guideline: Policies and procedures that detail how equipment is to be used, maintained and repaired and the level of personnel training required to use the equipment are implemented.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated individual supervises the management of medical equipment in the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: The responsibilities of this individual include as a minimum: Ensuring that the required medical equipment as listed in the standard intent above is available in the occupational health service Audits on available medical equipment (for example to ensure that it is correctly used, calibrated, stored etc.) Compiling an inventory of medical equipment, which includes description, make, manufacturer, model, serial number, tracing number, date of purchase, purchase price, list of accessories, supplier details, guarantee expiry dates and commissioning information or certificates Regular inspections of medical equipment Testing of medical equipment, as appropriate to its use and requirements Ensuring planned preventative maintenance of equipment

criteria.criterion_text: There is an inventory of the medical equipment available at the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: The supply of medical equipment is adequate to meet the needs of the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by observation and during personnel and client/worker/employee interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Records are kept of the checking and maintenance of medical equipment.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented procedure known to the personnel for reporting defects in medical equipment.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during personnel interviews document review..

standards.standard_title: Facility maintenance is managed to ensure the provision of a safe and effective service.
standards.intent: Management ensures that sufficient, competent personnel are available to manage routine and emergency functions and meet the needs of a safe and effective health service. Personnel may be in the employ of the occupational health service or be contracted out. Where there are contracted personnel, there must be clearly specified contracts, outlining their roles and responsibilities.

criteria.criterion_text: Written policies and procedures guide occupational health service personnel on the implementation of all maintenance service requirements.
criteria.guideline: This criterion is assessed based on the evidence of effective maintenance found throughout the occupational health service during the survey. If this criterion is scored PC or NC, the transgressions need to be recorded in detail to motivate for the PC/NC rating and it should be based on accurate facts.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated, competent individual is responsible for supervising the maintenance of buildings, grounds and utilities.
criteria.guideline: If this role is fulfilled by the occupational health service manager or solo practitioner, compliance will be measured on evidence of effective maintenance of the buildings, grounds and utilities. Where a designated individual has been identified to fulfil this role, compliance will be measured in accordance with the requirements set out in the employees/client’s position description.

criteria.criterion_text: Where these services are outsourced the occupational health service personnel have access to a list of these private contractors/service providers with their contact numbers at all times.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured on documented evidence and during personnel interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Written agreements ensure technical back-up services are available at all times during the opening or operational hours of the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Basic maintenance equipment, tools and spare parts are available.
criteria.guideline: The type and number of items available will vary in each occupational health service according to the size of the facility and the services offered but should be suitable and sufficient to prevent unnecessary risk to employees/clients, visitors and personnel or cessation of services due to deficiencies that are easily rectifiable.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service implements a documented preventative planned maintenance programme for buildings, grounds and utilities.
standards.intent:
The occupational health service plans for regular in-house inspection of facilities to avoid hazards.
Building maintenance includes the monitoring of the following aspects:
The general appearance of the inside and outside structure which includes the construction of walls, floors, doors and windows.
The condition of the paintwork
Water leaks, mould spots
Electrical wiring, for example exposed wires, switches, electrical sockets
Maintenance of the grounds (no litter, neat garden and grass kept short

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service plans and budgets for the upgrading or replacing of systems, buildings or components needed for the continued operation of a safe and effective facility.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided for example in the budget and/or strategic plan.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has a documented preventative maintenance management plan in place.
criteria.guideline: Where the occupational health service is a tenant, parts of the preventative maintenance plan may be the function of the landlord. There must however still be documented evidence provided to the surveyors to demonstrate that the landlord has a preventative maintenance plan for the facility. This will allow the surveyors to assess the responsibilities of the landlord and the responsibilities of the occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented inspections of the buildings and grounds are conducted at regular intervals as determined by the occupational health service policy.
criteria.guideline: The policy must include at least a) – e) in the standard intent above. Documented evidence will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented procedure known to the personnel for reporting defects.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during personnel interviews and availability of the procedure.

standards.standard_title: Information and communication technology (ICT) equipment is available and properly maintained to meet the needs of the service.
standards.intent:
The occupational health service is responsible for ensuring that appropriate ICT equipment is available and ready for use at all times. There is an accountable, systematic approach to ensuring that cost-effective, safe and appropriate equipment is available to meet the demands of quality employee/client care.
Managers take responsibility for ensuring that ICT equipment is available and appropriately maintained and that personnel are competent to use it.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures guide the management of ICT equipment are implemented.
criteria.guideline: All key processes that relate to the management of ICT equipment should be identified and the correct manner of performing the tasks documented. The purpose of this requirement is to ensure that important tasks are performed correctly and consistently.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated individual supervises the management of ICT equipment in the occupational health service.
There is an inventory of all ICT equipment.
criteria.guideline: This does not have to be a separate inventory, it can form part of a single inventory document for the occupational health service. All ICT equipment must be included in the inventory (printers, laptop computers, desktop computers etc.)

criteria.criterion_text: All desktop and server computers are provided with surge protection and the server is protected by an uninterruptable power supply.
criteria.guideline: Compliance with this criterion will ensure that data is not lost due to loss of power and that ICT equipment is not damaged, with potential loss of data, due to power surges.

criteria.criterion_text: A documented policy is available clearly describing appropriate back up procedures for electronic records.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: Regular checks are made and documented to ensure that backup has been successful.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: Records are kept of the checking and maintenance of ICT equipment.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has appropriate and up to date virus protection software and firewall protection to ensure adequate security and confidentiality of employee/client related information.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Documented evidence will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: There is documented evidence that relevant personnel are regularly trained to use/operate ICT equipment.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

categories.category_title: Employee/client record and personal information safety

standards.standard_title: A system for the storage, retrieval, retention and destruction of health records and personal information that meets the need for confidentiality and safety is implemented.
standards.intent:
Policies and procedures as well as managerial supervision ensure the safety and confidentiality of employee/client records and personal information.
The occupational health service develops and implements a policy that guides the storage, retrieval, retention and destruction of employee/client records. Employee/client records and other data and information are retained for sufficient periods to comply with law and regulation and support employee/client care, the management of the occupational health service, legal documentation, research and education. The retention policy is consistent with the confidentiality and security of such information. When the retention period is complete, employee/client records and other data and information are destroyed appropriately.
The policy will define:
Levels of access for individual personnel members
The user's obligation to keep information confidential
The process followed when confidentiality and/or security are violated
Secure storage and retrieval of paper based and electronic employee/client records and personal information
The protection of records against damage, fire, flood, theft, loss and electronic failure
The arrangements for succession of employee/client records in the event of closure of the occupational health service or death of the practitioner
The criteria for selection and method of destruction of employee/client records and personal information
Personnel members responsible for health record management must have suitable training and experience.
Employee/client records must be readily available each time the employee/client visits the occupational health service and therefore must be filed in such a way that they are easily identified.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures for the storage, retrieval, retention, destruction and confidentiality and safety of employee/client records and personal information are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Policies and procedures must include at least a) – g) in the standard intent above.

criteria.criterion_text: Designated individuals are responsible for the storage, maintenance and retrieval of employee/client files.
criteria.guideline: Only those designated individuals may have access to the employee/client records. There must be evidence of signed confidentiality agreements with each individual who has access to employee/client information.

criteria.criterion_text: The filing system allows for incorrectly filed records to be easily identified (for example, through colour coding of the records).
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Storage space for health records is sufficient and secure against unauthorised entry.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Records that have been removed from the designated storage area for use are kept out of public view.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: The retention process provides the necessary confidentiality and security.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Compliance will be measured by observation.

standards.standard_title: The cleaning and laundry service is managed to ensure the provision of a safe and effective service.
standards.intent:
Occupational health service managers must ensure that a documented policy is available detailing the cleaning and laundry duties to be undertaken and the frequency with which these need to be performed. Where these services are outsourced a contract defines the details of the service to be provided.
The occupational health service manager must ensure that facilities and equipment are adequate for the provision of a safe and effective cleaning and laundry service.

criteria.criterion_text: Written policies and procedures relating to cleaning and laundry duties and the frequency with which these duties are carried out are implemented and monitored.
criteria.guideline: Policies and procedures must detail how the core functions are to be performed. Implementation of these policies and procedures should be monitored to ensure consistent service delivery according to agreed standards of practice. Compliance will be verified by documented evidence of monitoring activities and by observation of implementation of the policies.

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence that laundry used for employee/clients is washed separately from domestic laundry.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during personnel interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Adequate, secure and well-ventilated storage areas are available for cleaning and laundry equipment and chemicals.
criteria.guideline: Chemicals for cleaning and laundry are safely stored in a well-ventilated, locked cupboard or room out of the reach of employee/clients, children and visitors. There is adequate storage place for brooms and mops.

criteria.criterion_text: Mops and brooms are cleaned and dried before being stored.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service manager ensures that cleaning and laundry personnel are appropriately trained regarding waste management, infection control procedures, confidentiality issues and any other relevant matters.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of such training will be required.

standards.standard_title: Where contracts/service agreement exist for clinical, managerial or hotel services (cleaning, laundry, food) they provide for adequate number of suitably trained contract personnel to provide a safe and effective service.
standards.intent:
Where support services have been contracted to outside agencies such as cleaning, building maintenance, gardening, ICT management, etc., the occupational health service leaders must ensure supervision of these services to ensures that they comply with the terms of the contract, meet employee/client needs and are monitored as part of the quality management and improvement activities.
The occupational health service manager identifies the number of personnel required for contracted services and defines the desired education, knowledge, skills and any other requirements needed.
Orientation and induction programmes ensure the competence of personnel before they begin to carry out their functions. Contract personnel must act in accordance with position descriptions and are evaluated in accordance with their assigned responsibilities.
Where appropriate, personnel in the employ of the contractor are made aware of issues relating to infection control, waste management, confidential waste and health and safety requirements.

criteria.criterion_text: Copies of contracts for outside service providers are available to those who ensure they are implemented.
criteria.guideline: It is very important that those who are required to monitor compliance with a contract have access to the deliverable elements of the document. The criterion does not require that leaders share the financial details of the contract. Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Services provided under contracts and other arrangements are formally monitored and compliance with the contract is documented.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Contracted personnel are managed as determined in the written service agreement.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service ensures that contracted personnel are oriented to the occupational health service and to relevant occupational health service policies and procedures.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of orientation to both the facility, the position and relevant policies and procedures is required.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service ensures that contracted personnel participate in relevant in-service training programmes.
criteria.guideline: In-service training must include for example, infection control, health and safety, fire and evacuation etc. A record must be kept of such training and must include all contracted personnel.

criteria.criterion_text: OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH SERVICES AND EMPLOYEE/CLIENT CARE
OVERVIEW OF  OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH SERVICES AND EMPLOYEE/CLIENT CARE

The main purpose of an occupational health service is to provide healthcare services to employee/clients. Providing appropriate care in an environment that supports and responds to each employee/client's unique needs requires a high level of planning and coordination. Certain activities are basic to employee/client care, such as planning and delivering appropriate care to each employee/client, monitoring the employee/client's response to the care provided, modifying care when necessary and completing the follow-up.
Throughout all phases of care, employee/client needs are matched with appropriate resources within and, when necessary, outside the occupational health service.

Processes for continuity and coordination of care among physicians, nurses and other healthcare providers must be implemented in and between all services. These processes should be designed collaboratively and implemented by the leaders of the various settings and services to ensure coordination of care.
Standards

standards.standard_title: In consultation with the employee/client, the occupational health service provides care that is consistent with best available evidence and in accordance with employee/client needs and values.
standards.intent:
Clinical guidelines should be selected and agreed by all clinicians. Consideration should be given to providing guidelines for high risk, high volume and high cost conditions. In addition, guidelines should be available for conditions which are rarely seen but may have severe consequences for employee/clients if misdiagnosed or mismanaged, for example, malaria in a nonendemic area.
Guidelines are found in the literature under many names including practice parameters, practice guidelines, employee/client care protocols, standards of practice, care pathways, etc. National guidelines for selected conditions are further provided by the Ministry of Health and Wellness. Regardless of the source, the scientific basis of guidelines should be reviewed and approved by the clinical leaders and clinical practitioners before implementation. This will ensure that they meet the criteria established by the leaders and are adapted to the community, employee/client needs and occupational health service resources.
Once adopted, guidelines should be reviewed on a regular basis to ensure their continued relevance. Consistency in the approach to diagnosis and management of care among those who are involved in the clinical care of an individual employee/client is an important aspect of continuity of care. Employee/clients value consistency in the quality of treatment they receive from an occupational health service and expect that treatment and advice given by different practitioners within the service will not be in conflict.
Clinical practice guidelines provide important recommendations for clinical care and should be accessible at the point of care. Occupational health services need to check that clinical practice guidelines are current.

criteria.criterion_text: The clinical team uses current clinical guidelines relevant to the occupational health service, to assist in the diagnosis and management of employee/clients.
The clinical team set criteria to select clinical practice guidelines.
criteria.guideline: Every effort should be made to identify and adopt guidelines that have been rigorously and scientifically developed, internationally accepted and adapted for local use.

criteria.criterion_text: The clinical team can describe how they ensure consistency of diagnosis and management of employee/clients.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during personnel interviews and the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The clinical team can demonstrate how they communicate about clinical issues and support systems in the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during personnel interviews and the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: Employee/clients are informed of the range of services and the processes to access the occupational health service.
standards.intent: Employee/clients and services working in collaboration with the occupational health service need to know how and when to contact the occupational health service to access care. It is reasonable to expect most occupational health services to offer care during normal office hours.

criteria.criterion_text: When employee/clients register with the occupational health service they are informed of the range of services offered by the service, opening hours, contact details and after hours' care arrangements.
criteria.guideline: It is advisable to provide this information in printed form as employee/clients may not remember what has been told. Employee/clients must acknowledge receipt of the information by signing (on the registration form) that they have been informed.

criteria.criterion_text: Where the occupational health service has an "on hold" telephone message, it includes a message for an alternative number to be used in an emergency.
criteria.guideline: This criterion is only applicable to occupational health services where the telephone system diverts to an ‘on hold’ message when the line is busy during business hours. The purpose of this requirement is to assist employee/clients who are dealing with an emergency during peak business hours, when it might be difficult to get through to the practice.  If the occupational health service does not have an ‘on hold’ system this criterion will be not applicable.

criteria.criterion_text: A message on the occupational health service's telephone answering machine, call diversion system or paging system provide information to employee/clients on how to obtain care outside the occupational health service’s normal opening hours.
criteria.guideline: The intention of this criterion is to ensure that employee/clients are assisted with the relevant information outside of business hours.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service renders services based on the needs of the population, during the hours published.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence can be provided by time cards, duty rosters etc. and can also be verified during employee/client and personnel interviews.

standards.standard_title: There is a process for appropriate referral of employee/clients for specialised consultation/investigations at other healthcare facilities.
standards.intent:
In some cases, practitioners refer employee/clients for a secondary consultation to confirm an opinion, to request more extensive diagnostic evaluations than may be available to the practitioner or to have employee/clients receive specialised treatment that the referring occupational health service may be unable to provide. The occupational health service must clearly describe the referral process. Occupational health services may wish to consider the use of a standard referral form which includes a tear off slip for the receiving doctor's response following the consultation.
The referral must contain the following as a minimum:
a)   At least three approved employee/client identifiers
Relevant history, examination findings and current management
Known allergies, adverse drug reactions and current medication
The name of the referring doctor
The name of the doctor/service referred to
Stated purpose of the referral
Request for feedback following the consultation with the specialist

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented process to refer employee/clients.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The lines of communication between the occupational health service, referral hospital and community services are clearly defined.
criteria.guideline: Occupational health service personnel must clearly describe the referral process, especially where employee/clients are sent to another healthcare organisation for specialist consultation or special investigations and then return to the referring occupational health service for continuation of treatment. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The practitioner informs the employee/client if he/she has any financial interest in the referral service.
criteria.guideline: Surveyors must pay particular attention to services that are offered in the same facility by different practitioners for example, where an occupational health practitioner refers an employee/client to a radiology or laboratory practice in the same facility.

criteria.criterion_text: Referrals are to specific individuals and/or agencies in the employee/client's home community wherever possible.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/clients and, as appropriate, their families are given follow-up instructions which are provided in an understandable form and manner.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: A copy of the referral note is available in the employee/client record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The referral is made on appropriate stationery or electronically.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The referral contains as a minimum a) – g) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service keeps a record of replies received following the referral of employee/client.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided of the system that is used in the occupational health service to record and act upon replies received from referral practitioners.

criteria.criterion_text: There are written guidelines for the referral of employee/clients in an emergency.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Follow-up care based on the findings of investigations/consultations performed outside the occupational health service are noted in the employee/client record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has an appointment system and a process for recognising cases requiring more urgent attention and providing employee/clients with longer appointments when appropriate.
standards.intent:
For efficiency of service delivery, appointment systems are recommended as best practice. The system must be flexible in determining the order in which employee/clients are seen to accommodate the employee/client' needs for urgent care, non-urgent care, complex care, planned chronic disease management, preventive healthcare and longer consultations.
Members of the occupational health service team should be sensitive to the need for longer consultations when the need for a longer consultation could be anticipated (for example, when the employee/client is attending for multiple or complex problems, chronic disease management or procedures).
Continuity of care is best achieved when an employee/client has on-going care from the same clinician. The healthcare practitioner-employee/client relationship is central to the provision of care. In recognition of this, employee/clients are offered an appointment with their preferred clinician when possible.
If the occupational health service is involved in training or research which makes it necessary for third parties to be present during the consultation, the employee/client is informed of this and given the opportunity to offer or withhold consent.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a flexible appointment system for consultations.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will be scored compliant in occupational health services that do not utilise an appointment system if it can be demonstrated that employee/clients who request a consultation at a specific time (for example, because of business commitments, travel arrangements etc.) are accommodated.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented system for fast-tracking the very ill, and pregnant women.
criteria.guideline: Both clinical and administrative personnel must be able to describe the occupational health service’s system for fast-tracking employee/clients and the process for seeking urgent medical assistance from a clinical personnel member.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/clients who are waiting are advised of any delays that may be experienced in receiving attention.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured during employee/client and personnel interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a system to ensure that employee/clients are seen within the shortest possible time.
criteria.guideline: To be compliant with this criterion personnel must be able to describe the employee/client journey from arriving at the occupational health service until they leave. Administrative processes must be simple, standardised and streamlined as far as possible.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/clients are offered longer appointments when clinically indicated.
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service must identify those instances where experience has shown that longer appointments will be required. This criterion links to 3.2.3.4 above where employee/clients will not be seen in the shortest possible time if appointments run over time with preceding employee/clients.

criteria.criterion_text: The employee/client's consent is obtained if third parties are expected to be present during the consultation.
criteria.guideline: This can apply to students, researchers, other occupational health service personnel and even to the employee/client's family and care-givers. Documented evidence must be provided.

standards.standard_title: At registration, sufficient details are taken from the employee/client to ensure that the employee/client can be contacted by the occupational health service when necessary and that the practitioner seeing the employee/client for the first time has sufficient background information to provide adequate care to the employee/client.
standards.intent:
Accurate contact details are essential in order to follow up results, recall patients for chronic disease monitoring and contact patients or nominated next of kin in emergency situations that may arise. Background medical information is essential to the provision of adequate care, and should be obtained in the first instance.
The following details should be obtained at registration of a new patient as a minimum:
Name
Date of birth
Identification numbers
Gender
Current address
Telephone numbers
Next of kin
Who to contact in an emergency
Previous medical history
Previous surgical history
Current medication (prescribed and over the counter medications)
Allergies
Immunisations
Health risk factors, for example, smoking, alcohol consumption, physical activity
Patient/guardian signature

criteria.criterion_text: New employee/clients to the occupational health service are asked to complete a form detailing at least a) – o) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/clients are asked to update the occupational health service if their contact details change.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Health funder details are accurately recorded for each new employee/client when required.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: Employee/clients can obtain advice or information related to their clinical care by telephone and electronic means.
standards.intent:
Where employee/clients, who are known to an occupational health practitioner, request information or follow up regarding a condition for which they have previously consulted, and the practitioner determines that it is clinically safe not to review the employee/client in a face to face consultation, the employee/client has access to the relevant information by telephone or electronic communication. The occupational health service has a documented policy regarding such communication that clearly outlines:
a)      Limitations of use
Positive identification of the employee/client
Timeframe for a response from a clinician
That the employee/client was made aware of any costs involved
Documentation of the communication in the employee/client record

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service implements a policy on telephonic and electronic communication with employee/clients that details at least a) – e) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during personnel and employee/client interviews, employee/client record audits and by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence of occupational health practitioner/employee/client telephone or electronic advice and information in the employee/client health records.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service can demonstrate how it receives and returns telephone and electronic messages from employee/clients.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has a system for the follow up and review of tests and results.
standards.intent:
The information gained from tests and results can have a considerable impact on the choices employee/clients and occupational health practitioners make in employee/client care. There may be considerable risk in not following up clinically significant tests and results. There is a system to track all results and where potentially serious pathology is suspected, practitioners have a system to track that the investigation is completed and the result is received and acted upon.
To minimise clinical risk, results of investigations are reviewed by the occupational health practitioner, signed or initialled (or the electronic equivalent), acted on in a timely manner and incorporated into the employee/client health record.
Many occupational health services provide the results of investigations to their employee/clients by telephone. The person responsible for giving the results should ensure that the recipient of the information is correctly identified using three employee/client identifiers so that employee/client confidentiality is not compromised. Acceptable employee/client identifiers are name, date of birth, address, identification number and employee/client number.

criteria.criterion_text: A documented policy on the review and management of pathology results, imaging reports, investigation reports and other clinical correspondence received by the occupational health service is implemented.
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service needs a documented system for the follow up of all tests and results with a strong focus on risk management. This policy must include that there is documented evidence that every report and result was seen, reviewed and acted upon by an occupational health practitioner.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/client health records contain evidence that all pathology results, imaging reports, investigation reports and clinical correspondence received by the occupational health service have been handled in accordance with occupational health service policy.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record and occupational health service documentation audit.

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence of review of the results of procedures and diagnostic tests performed by an occupational health practitioner.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a system to advise employee/clients of the process to follow up on results.
criteria.guideline: Employee/clients must be encouraged to take responsibility for their own health. The occupational health service must therefore provide the employee/client with information about what to expect following any investigation. For example, how long it takes to get the results, the method by which the employee/client will be informed of the results etc. Compliance will be verified during employee/client interviews and employee/client record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: A documented system to identify, follow up and recall employee/clients with clinically significant results is implemented.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  The system must be implemented in such a way that an employee/client who is not immediately contactable remains flagged for follow up. Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: When employee/clients are informed of results over the telephone, the employee/client is identified with 3 approved identifiers before the information is given.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: When results are given over the telephone, the result is given to the employee/client themselves or the employee/client’s consent is obtained before giving the result to a nominated third party.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: Employee/client and family education promotes the concept of taking responsibility for one's own health care.
standards.intent:
Every employee/client is offered the information and education he or she requires. All clinical personnel within the occupational health service work collaboratively to provide education in a coordinated manner. Personnel collaboration helps to ensure that the information employee/clients and families receive is comprehensive, consistent and as effective as possible.
Education is focused on the specific knowledge and skills the employee/client and his or her family will need to make care decisions, participate in care and continue care at home. Variables such as educational literacy, beliefs and limitations are considered. Each occupational health service decides on the placement and format of educational assessment, planning and delivery of information. Education regarding high risk health issues relevant to the local population is routinely provided by the occupational health service. Standardised materials and processes are used where possible.
Information provided by the occupational health service may include when to resume daily activities, preventive occupational health services relevant to the employee/client's condition and, when appropriate, information on coping with disease or disability.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service offers relevant health education to its employee/clients in a planned and consistent manner which enables employee/clients to meet their on-going health needs and achieve their health goals.
criteria.guideline: This will be assessed based on the evaluation of the employee/client education activities in relation to the services offered as well as the individual employee/client education during consultation, for example, lifestyle advice, when to seek medical attention - how urgently and at what level of care, etc. Compliance will be verified during employee/client interviews and employee/client record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: Posters and pamphlets regarding high risk and commonly treated conditions relevant to the occupational health service population (for example, HIV, TB, Diabetes, Hypertension) are visibly displayed and accessible in the waiting room and routinely provided to employee/clients during the consultation when appropriate.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during employee/client interviews, employee/client record audits and by observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Confidential HIV counselling and screening services are offered.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/client and family education provided is noted in the employee/client record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service identifies community resources which support continuing health promotion and disease prevention education and has contact details of these resources available.
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service must identify those resources that are specific to its employee/client population for example, breastfeeding support, mental health support, diabetes society etc. Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: There is documented evidence that employee/clients are referred to these resources, where appropriate.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: Information is provided in a manner and in a language that is understood by those making the care decisions.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during employee/client interviews.

standards.standard_title: Employee/client records contain the required information for the relevant condition.
standards.intent:
Medical records are intended to support employee/client care and should authentically represent every consultation (including by telephone and electronic mail). The clinical record of each employee/client, whether handwritten or electronic, needs to contain sufficient information to support the diagnosis and justify the treatment provided. It also needs to document the care given and the course and results of treatment. A standardised format and content of an employee/client's record will help promote the integration and continuity of care among the various providers of care to the employee/client. The occupational health service determines the specific data and information recorded in the clinical record, but it contains as a minimum for each consultation:
a)   The date of the consultation
Who conducted the consultation
Reason for the consultation
Relevant clinical findings including documenting the presence or absence of jaundice, anaemia, cyanosis, clubbing, oedema and lymphadenopathy (JACCOL)
Vital signs, including weight and height
Diagnosis
Recommended management plan and, where appropriate, expected process of review
Any medication prescribed for the employee/client (including name, strength, directions for use/dose frequency, number of repeats and date medication started/ceased/changed)
Complementary medication used by the employee/client
All requested investigations and procedures
Any relevant preventive care undertaken
Any referral to other healthcare providers or health services
Any special advice or other instructions
Follow up instructions given to the employee/client
Health education
For emergency cases, relevant times are recorded, i.e. time seen, time drugs administered, etc.
Abbreviations and symbols are standardised. Such standardisation is consistent with recognised local and national standards.
Employee/client contact details are kept up to date.
All relevant costs are discussed with employee/clients prior to these costs being incurred and the discussion is documented in the employee/client record.

criteria.criterion_text: Notes for each consultation contain as a minimum points a) – p) above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Handwritten notes are legible.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Notes are recorded contemporaneously or instantly.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: All abbreviations are standardised in accordance with recognised local and national standards.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The employee/client' records, including contact details, are up to date to ensure the transfer of the latest information between care providers.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Adverse drug reactions are noted in the employee/client's record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The employee/client is fully informed regarding the estimated costs of any treatment, investigation, procedure or referral and the discussion is documented.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service ensures that all notice of death forms are completed correctly.
standards.intent:
Information from notice of death forms is used to monitor mortality trends in the population which in turn is used to inform planning, monitoring and evaluation of health service provision. It is therefore of national importance that good quality information is provided to ensure that health needs are accurately identified and met.
Notice of death forms should contain as a minimum:
a)   Immediate cause of death
All identifiable intermediate causes of death
Underlying cause of death (essential)
Any comorbidities suffered by the employee/client which are contributing causes of death (not the underlying cause or intermediate causes of death in Part 1)
No abbreviations are used
The handwriting is legible
Any relevant occupational history is recorded
Specifics of cancers and infections must be provided

criteria.criterion_text: Notice of death forms are completed correctly containing at least details a) – h) above.
criteria.guideline: This whole standard will be scored not applicable where no notice of death form has been completed during the preceding survey period. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Where the death was not exclusively due to natural causes, the death is referred to the police or forensic pathologist and the referral is recorded in the employee/client record along with the name, position title and contact details of the person to whom the referral has been made.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  This whole standard will be scored not applicable where no notice of death form has been completed during the preceding survey period. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Copies of notice of death forms completed by the occupational health service are filed in the employee/client records.
criteria.guideline: This whole standard will be scored not applicable where no notice of death form has been completed during the preceding survey period. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Dated entries in the employee/client record reflect the date the notice of death form was collected and the name of the person to whom the notification was given.
criteria.guideline: This whole standard will be scored not applicable where no notice of death form has been completed during the preceding survey period. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The death notification form is made available for collection within legislated time frames.
criteria.guideline: This whole standard will be scored not applicable where no notice of death form has been completed during the preceding survey period. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Continuity of care

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has an effective clinical handover system that ensures safe and continuing healthcare delivery for employee/clients.
standards.intent:
Clinical handover needs to occur whenever there is an interface of care by different providers.
Examples of clinical handover include:
An occupational health practitioner covering for a fellow occupational health practitioner who is on leave or is unexpectedly absent
An occupational health practitioner covering for a part time colleague
An occupational health practitioner handing over care to another health professional such as an occupational health service nurse physiotherapist, podiatrist or psychologist
An occupational health practitioner referring an employee/client to a service outside the occupational health service
A shared care arrangement (for example, team care of an employee/client with mental health problems)
After hours' care providers are at a disadvantage with regards to specific groups of employee/clients requiring special attention such as chronically ill employee/clients. To ensure optimum care provision and a consistent approach from all practitioners, it is to the advantage of both employee/client and practitioner if relevant information is made available to the practitioners providing after hours' care.
The sharing of information relating to infection control (for example, HIV, TB, Hep B, MRSA) will enable those providing care outside the occupational health service (after hours, secondary care, etc.) to offer the employee/client and persons coming into contact with the affected employee/client appropriate care and enable them to take precautionary measures.
To ensure the transfer of this information, the employee/client is provided with a letter to present to clinicians providing care outside the occupational health service.

criteria.criterion_text: There is implementation of a documented policy on clinical handover to ensure that standard processes are followed.
criteria.guideline: The complexity and comprehensiveness of the content of handover information required to achieve continuity of care will differ for different types of handover situations. The details to be included in each situation should be agreed upon by all stakeholders and formalised to ensure a standardised approach at all times. The transfer of information must be documented, preferably using standardised forms (electronic or paper-based) for the various handovers. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/clients share in decision making regarding the handing over of clinical information.
Handover of clinical information is recorded in the employee/client' health records.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Information relating to those employee/clients requiring special attention, such as the chronically ill or those with infections which require specific infection control measures, is provided in an employee/client held letter, a copy of which is kept in the employee/client record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service ensures safe and reasonable arrangements for medical care for employee/clients outside of normal opening hours.
standards.intent:
Medical care outside normal opening hours needs to be provided by recognised medical practitioners.
The method of providing such care can be achieved in one of the following ways:
The occupational health service's practitioners provide their own care for employee/clients outside normal opening hours, either individually or through a roster
Formal arrangements for cooperative care outside the normal opening hours exist through a cooperative of one or more local occupational health services
Formal arrangements exist with a medical deputising (locum) service
Formal arrangements exist with a local hospital or after hours' facility
When the occupational health service's practitioners cannot safely or reasonably deliver care outside normal opening hours themselves, the occupational health service can clearly document the alternative system of care that is available for their employee/clients.
The occupational health service is responsible for ensuring the alternative care provider offers care to an acceptable standard.

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence of one (or a combination) of arrangements for employee/clients to access care outside of normal opening hours.
criteria.guideline: Regardless of the arrangements used to provide care outside normal opening hours, documented evidence of the system the occupational health service uses to provide such care needs to be available.

criteria.criterion_text: The credentials of alternative care providers are verified.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will be not applicable where employee/clients are referred to public and private hospitals after hours. Evidence must be provided that the credentials of locums and practitioners in partner occupational health services have been verified.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/client health records contain reports or notes of consultations occurring outside normal opening hours by or on behalf of the occupational health service.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will not be applicable to consultations that occur at public health facilities. Evidence must be provided on how and when the occupational health service receives notes of consultations occurring through all other after-hours arrangements. Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service provides emergency treatment and care.
standards.intent: The arrival of emergency employee/clients may be unpredictable, particularly in occupational health services that do not form part of a 24-hour service. The occupational health service must be prepared to provide primary emergency treatment as required to stabilise an employee/client while waiting for further assistance from emergency medical services.

criteria.criterion_text: Written guidelines are available and followed relating to the provision of primary emergency services.
criteria.guideline: As a minimum, the Basic Life Support algorithm must be available and followed. Documented evidence of training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Guidelines for emergency triage, assessment and treatment are available and followed for all types of employee/clients seen at the occupational health service (for example adult, paediatric and neonatal).
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service's policy on triage must make distinction between triage undertaken by members of the clinical team and triage undertaken by personnel with non-clinical roles. Appropriate training must be provided to assist both administrative personnel and members of the clinical team to identify employee/clients in need of urgent care. Documented evidence of training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Information on cases and the outcome of emergency treatment are recorded in a register/logbook.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Case reviews are undertaken within the occupational health service to assess the quality of treatment and care of employee/clients requiring emergency care.
criteria.guideline: This can take the form of morbidity and mortality meetings or debriefing sessions. Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a protocol that delineates how the occupational health service evaluates, manages, stabilises and transfers employee/clients with emergency conditions.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service provides resuscitation in accordance with occupational health service policy.
standards.intent:
Resuscitation policies and equipment should be standardised throughout the occupational health service.
Standardised resuscitation processes require:
The level at which resuscitation is provided and by whom
Coordination among those who provide and maintain the equipment
Availability of required equipment
Availability of required drugs
Initial and ongoing training of personnel in the use of equipment and execution of procedures
Maintenance and monitoring of equipment
Current, evidence-based guidelines for resuscitation
Deficiencies in the system regarding equipment, its use and the knowledge and skills required by those who carry out resuscitation should be identified, documented and acted upon. Each occupational health service should identify those members of personnel to be trained in emergency life support and the level of training (basic or advanced) appropriate to their role in the service. The person(s) providing the training must be currently registered and/or accredited with a recognised body as a resuscitation trainer. Training in many instances can be outsourced.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has an implemented resuscitation policy.
criteria.guideline: Documented policies and procedures that include at least a) – g) must be available and implemented. Evidence of implementation of all the listed policies is required for a compliance rating, otherwise this criterion will be scored PC.

criteria.criterion_text: All occupational health service personnel are trained in basic resuscitation techniques at least every two years, with records of their attendance at such training.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of nationally accepted training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: All medical practitioners are updated in medication protocols for resuscitation and resuscitation techniques at least every two years with records of their attendance at such training.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Documented evidence of nationally accepted training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated person documents monthly checks to ensure that resuscitation medication and equipment is available, in working order and that medication is not expired and reports any adverse findings for immediate remediation.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

standards.standard_title: Equipment for resuscitation is available in accordance with the policies of the occupational health service.
standards.intent:
Resuscitation equipment and medication is available at the point of need within 1 minute. In addition, there is access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within 3 minutes of any employee/client collapsing. Resuscitation equipment includes at least:
a)   A defibrillator/AED with adult and infant paddles/pads
An ECG monitor
A bag-mask manual ventilator
A selection of oropharyngeal airways
Equipment to perform an emergency cricothyroidotomy – (needle and surgical)
Appropriate facilities for intravenous therapy and drug administration
Drugs for cardiac arrest, coma, seizures and states of shock (including paediatric doses)
Plasma expanders

criteria.criterion_text: There is a designated resuscitation area.
criteria.guideline: This does not have to be an area that is used exclusively for resuscitation, it may be in the normal consulting area. The area must however allow easy access for resuscitation equipment and emergency personnel, have a suitable surface on which to perform resuscitation and ensure the privacy of the employee/client.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a mechanism for the summoning of medical help in an emergency.
criteria.guideline: This refers to the summoning of medical help both from within the occupational health service itself (to alert personnel that there is an emergency) and from outside the occupational health service. Various methods such as an alarm, intercom, alert button, telephone etc. acceptable. Personnel must be able to demonstrate the procedure to be followed when an emergency arises.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service provides resuscitation equipment according to the services provided.
criteria.guideline: Resuscitation equipment and documented evidence of the checking thereof according to the occupational health service's resuscitation policy must be available but must include at least a) – h) in the standard intent above. Checking must include the identification of expiry dates on consumables such as airways. Documented evidence of this checking is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Diagnostic and vital sign monitoring equipment is available as per occupational health service policy.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured on physical inspection.

criteria.criterion_text: Equipment for early cardiopulmonary resuscitation is available within one minute in each area of the occupational health service and access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within three minutes of any employee/client collapsing.
criteria.guideline: Resuscitation equipment must be available at the point of need within one minute. In addition, there must be access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within three minutes of any employee/client collapsing.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has access to Ambulance Services (EMS).
criteria.guideline: The occupational health service personnel must be able to describe the process for contacting ambulance services and the contact details must be readily available at all telephones in the occupational health service.

standards.standard_title: Prescribing and dispensing of medications adheres to laws, regulations and professional standards of occupational health service.
standards.intent:
The occupational health service must ensure that those responsible for prescribing, dispensing and ordering of medication are suitably qualified and registered.
The occupational health service dispenses medication in the most ready-to-administer form possible to minimise opportunities for error during reconstitution and administration. The system supports accurate dispensing of medication in a timely manner.
The occupational health service implements systems to ensure that all pharmaceutical occupational health services are in accordance with current legislation.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated individual who is suitably qualified has clearly defined responsibilities and accountability for all aspects of medication management.
criteria.guideline: his requires that an individual in the occupational health service has the officially assigned duties of overseeing and taking responsibility for all aspects of medication management. This may be the occupational health service manager or a suitably qualified health professional.
