criteria.criterion_text: Medication is prescribed and dispensed in accordance with legislation and current pharmaceutical, medical and nursing guidelines.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The scope of and limitations to the responsibilities and activities of the personnel who manage medication are clearly defined in written policies.
criteria.guideline: This is particularly relevant where certain medication may only be prescribed by designated specialists. Compliance will be verified with reference to occupational health service policy and country-specific legislation.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication is securely and legibly labelled with relevant information as required by occupational health service policy.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Country-specific regulations apply, but must include at least the following: Name of employee/client and employee/client record number (if applicable) Proprietary name/approved name or name of each active ingredient Direction with regard to the manner in which medication must be used Strength and number of dose units in the container Date of dispensing Expiry date and batch number Additional labels with warnings and storage instructions in accordance with occupational health service policy

criteria.criterion_text: A register is maintained of all medication dispensed.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: The person prescribing and dispensing the medication has access to employee/client information that would contraindicate prescription of particular medication.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The person dispensing medication informs the employee/client of available generic equivalents.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Controlled medication is prescribed and dispensed in accordance with legislation and occupational health service policy.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Compliance will be measured by examining the dispensing register, the controlled medication register and during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Verbal/telephonic medication orders are documented in the employee/client's record.
Prescriptions conform to all legal requirements.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has a nominated personnel member who is suitably trained to oversee cold chain management.
standards.intent:
The potency of medication depends on suitable storage. Vaccines, in particular, which are exposed to high ambient temperatures and/or freezing will quickly lose their potency. The cold chain is the system of transporting and storing vaccines and other temperature sensitive medication within the safe temperature range of 2 - 8°C. For vaccines to be effective the cold chain must be maintained from the place of manufacture to the point of administration. Each time that vaccines are exposed to the wrong temperature their potency is reduced. To know if vaccines are potent at the time of administration, it is important that they are monitored for exposure to heat and cold as they pass through the cold chain. While domestic refrigerators are not designed to meet the requirements of vaccine storage, safe storage is possible if healthcare facilities follow simple guidelines. Guidelines may be obtained from the health authorities or from the manufacturers and distributors of vaccines. Foodstuffs must not be stored in the medication refrigerator.
Deep freeze, refrigeration, cold room and cool area facilities are provided for safe storage of certain medications. There is a mechanism for ensuring that the correct temperature is maintained throughout the life of the medications. Deep freezers and refrigerators are defrosted when necessary. Doors, hinges and seals are all functional.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures are implemented to ensure that medication is transported to the occupational health service and stored within the service according to manufacturers’ guidelines, with specific emphasis on maintenance of cold chain requirements.
A dedicated refrigerator is available for medication requiring storage at low temperatures.
criteria.guideline: The refrigerator must be used exclusively for the storage of medication. The records should also reflect regular defrosting of the refrigerator with documented evidence of maintenance of the cold chain for medication during the defrosting process.

criteria.criterion_text: A monitoring log is kept of the temperature within the refrigerator and/or cold-chain monitors and is available for inspection.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Temperature records should demonstrate maintenance of the temperature between 2 and 8 degrees Celsius.

criteria.criterion_text: The fridge thermometer is calibrated at regular intervals determined by the occupational health service and the calibration is documented.
criteria.guideline: The calibration method will depend on the type of thermometer. Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Any remedial action taken is recorded.
criteria.guideline: When the refrigerator temperature is out of range, appropriate action should be taken and documented, and effectiveness of the action monitored. This will require evidence of action both in relation to the refrigerator and the medication stored in the refrigerator. Medication should be discarded when necessary according to manufacturer’s guidelines. Continued monitoring of refrigerator temperature should demonstrate return to the required range within an expected timeframe, failing which further action should be taken.

criteria.criterion_text: The integrity of door seals, door and hinges is checked at regular intervals and documented accordingly.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation and on documented evidence.

standards.standard_title: Medication is stored in a secure and clean environment.
standards.intent:
The occupational health service stores and dispenses medication in a clean and secure environment which complies with laws, regulations and professional practice standards. In particular, medication is clearly labelled, which includes the following:
Generic name
Strength of medicine
Dose, frequency and duration of course
Date of dispensing and expiry date
Name of employee/client
Name/address of supplier
Child safety warning
Batch number
Secure storage systems ensure that pharmaceuticals and related substances are held under conditions that conform to statutory requirements and manufacturer's requirements.
There are arrangements for ensuring the security of medication including alarm systems, door access controls and safes/vaults for storing controlled medicines.
There is a registry, log or other mechanism for monitoring and accounting for controlled substances.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication storage areas are protected from heat and light and effectively ventilated.
criteria.guideline: Note must be made of provision for medication that should not be exposed to direct sunlight. Where the ambient temperature in the cupboard/room cannot be maintained at 20-25 degrees Celsius there should be evidence that the environmental temperature is monitored and complies with the manufacturer’s requirements for medication storage.

criteria.criterion_text: Stored medication is legibly marked and securely labelled.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific regulations apply, but must include at least the following: Proprietary name/approved name or name of each active ingredient Strength and number of dose units in the container Expiry date and batch number

criteria.criterion_text: Controlled drugs are stored in a cabinet of substantial construction, for which only authorised personnel have the keys.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific laws and regulations and facility policy will determine the nature of such medication.

criteria.criterion_text: Controlled drugs are stored in suitable facilities which include but are not limited to: lockable storage facilities, ceiling cages, burglar guards and alarm systems with keypads.
criteria.guideline: The purpose of this criterion is to ensure the safety of controlled medication during times when the occupational health service is closed. Unlike 24-hour facilities, these unattended occupational health facilities may be targeted and therefore additional security measures are required.

criteria.criterion_text: Controlled drugs are accurately accounted for in a specific register which is updated contemporaneously and available for inspection.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Compliance will be measured against medication control regulations to ensure that the information in the register is accurate and complies with the requirements.

criteria.criterion_text: Controlled drugs that have expired are disposed of in accordance with legal requirements.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured against medication control regulations to ensure that the information in the register is accurate and complies with the requirements.

criteria.criterion_text: All pharmaceuticals, vaccines and medical consumables are regularly checked for expiry dates and checks are recorded.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured by observation and documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: An inventory management system either manual (stock cards) or automated, is in place to monitor maximum and minimum stock levels and control stock losses.
criteria.guideline: This requires the occupational health service to identify maximum and minimum stock levels based on records of medication use over time. Compliance will be measured by comparing the actual stock levels to the records.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has an employee/client rights policy.
standards.intent:
The leaders of the service are primarily responsible for the way in which its employee/clients are treated.
The leaders need to know and understand the employee/client's rights and the employer's responsibilities as specified in laws, charters and regulations. The leaders then provide direction to ensure that personnel throughout the occupational health service assume responsibility for protecting these rights. To protect and advance employee/client's rights effectively, the leaders work collaboratively and seek to understand their responsibilities in relation to its employee/clients.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/client rights are identified in a policy and documented in accordance with relevant and current laws, charters and regulations.
criteria.guideline: Employee/client and family rights are internationally accepted and implemented. Country specific laws, regulations and charters must be referenced in the policy. The policy must include at least the right to: A healthy and safe working environment Participation in decision-making Access to health care in the workplace Ethical health care Choice of health services Confidentiality and privacy Culturally sensitive care Informed consent Refusal of treatment A second opinion Complaints about health services

criteria.criterion_text: Documented evidence of personnel training in relation to these policies and procedures is available.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of such training can exist in various forms such as formal in-service training sessions, meetings where policies and procedures are discussed, personnel acknowledging in writing that they have studied policies and procedures, etc.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a employee/client rights charter which is prominently displayed, and employee/clients are made aware of their rights.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured on observation and by documented evidence that employee/clients are made aware of their rights. This can be achieved by drawing their attention to the poster or by having copies of the charter available.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service respects the rights of employee/clients and their families to refuse or discontinue treatment.
standards.intent:
Employee/clients or those making decisions on their behalf (i.e. in the case of minors or employee/clients who lack mental capacity due to physical or mental illness) may decide not to proceed with the planned care or treatment or not to continue care or treatment after it has been initiated. The occupational health service informs employee/clients and families about their right to make these decisions, about the potential outcomes that could result from these decisions and about their responsibilities related to such decisions. Employee/clients and families are given information on any care and treatment alternatives.
Personnel are informed of their responsibility to implement and respect the choices of employee/clients.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/clients are informed about their condition and the proposed treatment.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the employee/client record audit and/or employee/client interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service informs employee/clients and their families about their rights to refuse or discontinue treatment, and the consequences of such decisions.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the employee/client record audit and/or employee/client interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: When employee/clients refuse or discontinue treatment, the consultation regarding their decision is accurately and contemporaneously recorded and includes the details of the discussion regarding the consequences of the decision.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the employee/client record audit.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service takes measures to protect employee/client privacy.
standards.intent:
The occupational health service ensures that the employee/client's need for privacy is respected, especially when the employee/client is providing personal information and undergoing clinical examination. Employee/clients may desire privacy from other personnel, other employee/clients and even from family members.
Medical and other health information, when documented and collected in an employee/client record or other form, is important for understanding the employee/client, his or her needs and for providing care over time. The occupational health service respects such information as confidential and has implemented policies and procedures that protect such information from loss or misuse. The personnel respect the confidentiality of employee/client information by not leaving employee/client files, results, etc. where they might be visible to members of the public and by not holding employee/client-related discussions where they may be overheard by other employee/clients or visitors. Such carelessness with employee/client information can result in loss of dignity or employment for the employee/client and may result in damage to personal or family relationships. These consequences can follow carelessness by the personnel of the occupational health service, or by family members or others not authorised to have access to the information who have obtained information due to the carelessness of personnel.

criteria.criterion_text: The employee/client's need for privacy is protected during all examinations, procedures and treatments.
criteria.guideline: This must be evident in all employee/client care areas.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/client privacy is protected when providing personal information.
criteria.guideline: This must be evident in all employee/client care areas including administrative areas and waiting areas. Employee/client privacy should be safeguarded at all times, including when providing personal information to nonclinical personnel. It is not always necessary to provide privacy by means of physical barriers such as walls and doors. Distance between waiting employee/clients and those speaking with occupational health service personnel can also provide the required privacy when less sensitive information is being exchanged. For example, a line can be drawn on the floor some distance away from the registration desk or pharmacy counter. Employee/clients can be requested to wait behind the line and approach the counter only when providing their details or receiving their medication. Background noise, for example, music or employee/client education videos, can help to prevent conversations with employee/clients being overheard.

criteria.criterion_text: The employee/client's right to privacy is protected for all forms of counselling.
criteria.guideline: This must be evident in all employee/client care areas.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures to prevent the loss or misuse of employee/client information are implemented.
criteria.guideline: This criterion measures more than just the information contained in the employee/client record. The confidentiality agreement that is signed by personnel must include the protection of all employee/client related information including for example, discussing employee/client details with anyone not involved in the employee/client’s care, comments on social media etc. Compliance will be measured by evidence of a signed confidentiality agreement and by examining the incident management system in the occupational health service to establish if any complaints or incidents have been reported involving a breach of employee/client confidentiality.

criteria.criterion_text: When appropriate, employee/client are permitted to be accompanied by a family member/care-giver during consultations.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during employee/client interviews and observation.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service has a clearly defined process for obtaining consent.
standards.intent:
One of the main ways that employee/clients are involved in their care decisions is by granting informed consent. The employee/client must be provided with all information relating to planned care to enable him or her to make decisions. The consent process is clearly defined by the occupational health service in policies and procedures. Relevant laws and regulations are incorporated into the policies and procedures.
Informed consent for care sometimes requires that people other than or in addition to the employee/client be involved in decisions about the employee/client's care. This is especially true when the employee/client does not have the mental or physical capacity to make care decisions, when culture or custom designate that others make care decisions or when the employee/client is a child (i.e. under the age of legally being able to consent). When the employee/client cannot make decisions regarding his or her care a surrogate decision maker is identified. When someone other than the employee/client gives consent, that individual is noted in the employee/client's record.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining general consent for treatment.
criteria.guideline: This can be achieved for example by obtaining general consent for treatment from employee/clients when they register at the occupational health service. Compliance will be assessed during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The occupational health service has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining consent in the case of employee/clients who are unable to grant consent for themselves by way of mental or physical incapacity, which is in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be assessed during the employee/client record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented consent is obtained from employee/client for health information to be provided to a third party.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of compliance with this criterion may be found in different documents. For example, employee/clients may give consent for information to be shared with health funders as part of their membership registration. Consent is required whenever health information is shared for example with employers, family members or carers, lawyers and insurance companies.

criteria.criterion_text: Where occupational health service members use employee/client information for research, approval has been obtained from the relevant ethics committee and from employee/client themselves where required.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

standards.standard_title: The occupational health service informs employee/clients and their families about the processes which it has instituted to receive and act on complaints, conflicts and differences of opinion about employee/client care and the employee/client's right to participate in those processes.
standards.intent: Employee/clients have a right to voice complaints about their care and to have those complaints reviewed and where possible resolved. Decisions regarding care sometimes present questions, conflicts or other dilemmas for the occupational health service and the employee/client, family or other decision-makers. The occupational health service has established processes for seeking resolution to such dilemmas and complaints. The occupational health service identifies in policies and procedures those who need to be involved in the processes and how the employee/client and family participate.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented policy outlining the mechanism to allow for the hearing of complaints and how to act upon them which is implemented.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of implementation of the complaints procedure, for example, complaints log and the documentation relating to the investigation and resolution of complaints, must be made available for assessment.

criteria.criterion_text: Employee/clients are aware of their right to voice complaints and the processes by which to do so, internally as well as externally where applicable.
criteria.guideline: The complaints process must be prominently displayed in the occupational health service. Results obtained from employee/client interviews and observation will determine the level of compliance.

criteria.criterion_text: A nominated individual within the occupational health service is responsible for managing the complaints and ensuring that the complaints policy is implemented.
criteria.guideline: This should be a senior person in the occupational health service and is usually the solo practitioner or the occupational health service manager.

criteria.criterion_text: A nominated individual within the occupational health service is responsible to oversee the investigation of and response to the complaint.
criteria.guideline: Complainants must be kept informed of the progress of the investigation into the complaint. If there is a legitimate delay, they should be informed of the reason for the delay and provided with regular updates as to the progress of the management of their complaint. Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Complaints are monitored, and repetitions or patterns are identified.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of such monitoring must be available.

criteria.criterion_text: Any opportunities for improvement identified from the investigation of complaints are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.
